proposals.title: A Clinical-Microbiological Risk Model to Guide Diagnosis in Culture-Negative Endocarditis: Challenging the Exclusion of Histoplasma capsulatum Infection
proposals.motivation: Histoplasma endocarditis is a life-threatening infection with notoriously low blood culture sensitivity, leading to diagnostic delays and missed cases when negative cultures are incorrectly interpreted as ruling out active infection. This diagnostic gap is critical for immunocompromised patients, a population central to Deresinski's research, where untreated fungal endocarditis carries high mortality. A structured framework is needed to quantify infection risk despite culture negativity, integrating clinical and microbiological data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a risk-stratification model incorporating host immune status, echocardiographic findings, serological markers (e.g., Histoplasma antigen), and epidemiological exposure can identify patients with a high probability of active Histoplasma endocarditis despite negative blood cultures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective case-control study of patients with suspected infective endocarditis and negative routine blood cultures. Cases will be Histoplasma endocarditis confirmed by histopathology/valve culture. Controls will be patients with other confirmed non-Histoplasma causes or non-infectious pathology. Using pre-2026 methods, we will apply multivariable logistic regression to identify independent predictors, building a clinical prediction score. Model performance will be assessed via area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) and bootstrap validation, akin to quantitative risk assessment frameworks used in Deresinski's aerosol transmission work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide clinicians with a validated, practical tool to estimate the risk of Histoplasma endocarditis in culture-negative scenarios, directly informing decisions for advanced diagnostics (e.g., PCR, valve histology) and empiric antifungal therapy. It will shift the diagnostic paradigm from binary exclusion by culture to probabilistic risk assessment, potentially improving outcomes in a high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: While Deresinski's prior work compares antibiotic efficacy in bacteremia, this proposal uniquely applies a clinical-microbiological risk modeling approach to a culture-negative fungal syndrome, integrating immunocompromise (a key theme) with diagnostic strategy. It challenges a core clinical assumption using quantitative methods from infection risk assessment, a novel synthesis for this disease.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study to Define the Optimal Duration of Liposomal Amphotericin B Induction Therapy for Histoplasma Endocarditis: A Risk-Benefit Analysis
proposals.motivation: Histoplasma endocarditis is a rare but life-threatening manifestation of disseminated histoplasmosis, requiring prolonged antifungal therapy. Liposomal amphotericin B (L-AmB) is the cornerstone of induction therapy, but its optimal duration is undefined, leading to variable practice. Prolonged use increases the risk of significant nephrotoxicity and electrolyte wasting, while an abbreviated course may risk clinical failure and relapse, particularly in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.hypothesis: A shorter, defined duration of L-AmB induction therapy (e.g., 2 weeks) for Histoplasma endocarditis will be non-inferior to longer courses (e.g., 4-6 weeks) in achieving clinical and mycological success, while significantly reducing the cumulative incidence of nephrotoxicity and other treatment-limiting adverse events.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with proven Histoplasma endocarditis treated with L-AmB from 2005-2025. Data on treatment duration, clinical/microbiological outcomes, and toxicity (serum creatinine, potassium, magnesium) will be collected. The primary analysis will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to compare treatment success between duration groups, adjusting for confounders like immunocompromised status and surgical intervention. A quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA)-inspired model will integrate efficacy and toxicity data to identify the duration that maximizes the therapeutic index.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based recommendation for the duration of L-AmB induction in this rare infection, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will establish a framework for balancing efficacy and toxicity for other invasive fungal infections requiring protracted amphotericin B courses. The findings will help standardize care and improve patient safety by minimizing avoidable drug-related harm.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have focused on comparative efficacy of antifungals, none have specifically modeled the risk-benefit trade-off of treatment duration for Histoplasma endocarditis. This integrates Deresinski's expertise in clinical mycology and risk assessment, applying a QMRA-style analytical approach—previously used for aerosol transmission—to a novel clinical pharmacology question.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Immunological Interface: Pre-existing Immunosuppression versus Infection-Induced Dysfunction in Infective Endocarditis
proposals.motivation: Infective endocarditis (IE) is a severe infection with high morbidity and mortality, particularly in immunocompromised hosts. The relative contribution of pre-existing immunosuppression (as a risk factor) versus immune dysfunction directly caused by the IE infection itself remains poorly defined. Clarifying this 'cause versus effect' relationship is critical for risk stratification, prognostication, and potentially for guiding adjunctive immunomodulatory therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of patients with IE, particularly those with non-HACEK Gram-negative or fungal pathogens, have identifiable pre-existing immunologic deficits (e.g., anticytokine autoantibodies, functional neutrophil defects) that predispose to infection, and that the IE episode further perturbs specific immune pathways, creating a compounded immunosuppressive state.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of adult patients with definite IE. For each patient, we will perform a standardized immunologic evaluation at diagnosis (pre-existing state) and at 4-6 weeks post-treatment (recovery state). Assessments will include: 1) Screening for anticytokine autoantibodies (e.g., anti-GM-CSF, anti-IFN-γ) via ELISA, 2) Functional neutrophil assays (oxidative burst, phagocytosis), and 3) Multiplex cytokine profiling. Clinical and microbiological data (pathogen, source, complications) will be collected. We will compare immunologic profiles against a matched control group of patients with bacteremia without IE and use multivariate regression to identify independent predictors of severe outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will delineate the prevalence and spectrum of pre-existing immunologic defects in IE, moving beyond classical risk factors like prosthetic valves or injection drug use. It will provide the first longitudinal data on infection-induced immune dysfunction in IE. The findings will inform a new framework for patient classification based on immune phenotype, which could guide future trials of pathogen-directed or host-directed therapies.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Deresinski's prior work identified anticytokine autoantibodies in fungal infections and compared antibiotic efficacy in immunocompromised hosts, this proposal directly integrates those themes to mechanistically dissect the host immune state in a specific, high-mortality infection where the immune paradigm is currently oversimplified.

proposals.title: A Host-Response Validated Machine Learning Framework to Classify Plasma Microbial Cell-Free DNA Detections in Neutropenic Patients
proposals.motivation: Plasma microbial cell-free DNA sequencing (KT) is a sensitive diagnostic for neutropenic patients but cannot distinguish true infection from colonization or background, leading to diagnostic ambiguity and potentially inappropriate antimicrobial use. Current clinical adjudication lacks a biological gold standard, relying on imperfect surrogates like blood cultures. There is an urgent need for a validated, evidence-based framework to interpret KT results in this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating KT results with a composite host-response signature (proteomic and immunophenotypic) and expert adjudication will create a robust standard for true infection, enabling the development of a high-performance machine learning classifier to accurately categorize future KT detections.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll a multicenter cohort of neutropenic patients undergoing KT testing. For each KT detection, we will collect concurrent plasma for host-response proteomics (Olink panels) and peripheral blood for deep immunophenotyping (mass cytometry). A blinded expert panel will adjudicate each case. Using this composite standard, we will train a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost) to classify KT organisms, with features including microbial load, host protein levels, immune cell subsets, and clinical metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first biologically validated, probabilistic classification tool for KT results in neutropenia, directly addressing a critical clinical dilemma. It will reduce diagnostic uncertainty, guide antimicrobial stewardship, and establish a new paradigm for defining infection in immunocompromised hosts using multi-omics integration.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond clinical correlation alone by establishing a composite biological standard for infection. It uniquely integrates KT, deep host-response profiling, and machine learning to solve a fundamental problem in infectious disease diagnostics.

proposals.title: Deriving High-Specificity Diagnostic Thresholds for Invasive Fungal Disease: A Probabilistic Framework Linking Serum Biomarker Levels to Clinical Outcomes in Immunocompromised Hosts
proposals.motivation: Diagnosis of invasive fungal infections (IFIs) like invasive aspergillosis remains challenging, often relying on non-culture-based assays such as galactomannan. Current diagnostic strategies frequently require confirmatory testing or complex clinical/radiological criteria, which can delay therapy or lead to over-treatment. A framework that translates a single assay result into a probabilistic estimate of true infection, validated against hard clinical outcomes, would streamline diagnosis while safeguarding against false positives, directly addressing a critical need in the management of immunocompromised patients.
proposals.hypothesis: For a defined immunocompromised cohort, a quantitative, non-linear relationship exists between a single serum biomarker level (e.g., galactomannan index) and the probability of a subsequent clinical outcome consistent with proven/probable IFI, allowing the derivation of a high-specificity diagnostic threshold with a positive predictive value exceeding 95%.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised patients (e.g., hematologic malignancy, transplant) with suspected IFI who had serum galactomannan testing. The primary outcome is a composite of microbiologically proven or probable IFI per EORTC/MSG criteria. Using logistic regression with restricted cubic splines, we will model the probability of the outcome as a function of the initial galactomannan index. From this model, we will identify the index threshold that yields a ≥95% positive predictive value in the derivation cohort, then validate this threshold in a temporally distinct cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically validated, high-specificity diagnostic cutoff for galactomannan that can support a 'definite' IFI diagnosis without mandatory confirmatory testing. It will introduce a generalizable probabilistic framework for linking assay values to clinical outcomes, moving beyond arbitrary cutoffs. This directly informs antimicrobial stewardship by reducing diagnostic ambiguity and unnecessary antifungal use.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing approaches that rely on consensus criteria combining multiple tests, this method derives diagnostic certainty from the quantitative relationship between a single assay and real-world clinical outcomes. It formally integrates quantitative microbial risk assessment principles, used in environmental studies, into individual patient diagnostics.

proposals.title: Outcome-Based Validation of Molecular Biomarkers as Definitive Diagnostic Criteria for Gram-Negative Bacteremia in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: In clinical microbiology, definitive diagnosis of infection often requires concordance between a key molecular test (KT) and a standard microbiological test (SMT), like culture. This paradigm can delay diagnosis, especially when SMT is slow or yields false negatives due to prior antibiotics. In high-risk immunocompromised patients with suspected Gram-negative bacteremia, such delays directly impact mortality and complication rates. A new diagnostic framework is needed to expedite definitive diagnosis when KT specificity is sufficiently high.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific Gram-negative pathogens in immunocompromised hosts, a positive molecular biomarker (e.g., PCR for a resistance gene or species-specific target) has a positive predictive value (PPV) for true infection exceeding 95% when validated against long-term clinical outcomes, thereby obviating the need for confirmatory culture to define a 'definite' case.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised patients with bacteremia due to Enterobacterales species (e.g., Enterobacter cloacae, Klebsiella aerogenes) from 2020-2025. Patients will be stratified by diagnostic method: KT-positive/SMT-positive (reference), KT-positive/SMT-negative, and KT-negative/SMT-positive. The primary analysis will use multivariable logistic regression to compare 30-day all-cause mortality and infection-related complication rates (e.g., septic shock, metastatic infection) between groups, adjusting for severity and therapy. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis will identify KT signal thresholds (e.g., cycle threshold values) that maximize PPV for poor outcomes, defining a standalone diagnostic criterion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an empirical, outcome-driven framework for redefining diagnostic certainty in clinical microbiology, moving beyond test concordance. It will generate specific, evidence-based criteria where molecular tests alone can trigger definitive diagnosis and guide earlier targeted therapy. This directly addresses the urgent need for rapid diagnostic paradigms in managing infections in high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the oncology model of biomarker-led diagnosis to infectious diseases, validating tests against patient outcomes rather than culture concordance. It integrates Deresinski's expertise in bacteremia outcomes and immunocompromised hosts with modern molecular diagnostics to challenge a foundational microbiological principle.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Pre-emptive Probiotic Decolonization to Prevent Carbapenem-Resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae Bacteremia in High-Risk Neutropenic Patients
proposals.motivation: Febrile neutropenia in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with hematologic malignancies, carries high mortality and cost, with infections by multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CPKP) being a major driver. Pre-2026 clinical studies, including Dr. Deresinski's work, highlight the poor outcomes and escalating antibiotic failure associated with such bacteremias. Proactive strategies to reduce the reservoir of MDROs prior to immunosuppression are urgently needed to improve outcomes and curb healthcare expenditures.
proposals.hypothesis: A pre-emptive, ecologically-based decolonization strategy using defined probiotic consortia in high-risk patients prior to neutropenia-inducing therapy will be cost-effective compared to standard reactive care, by reducing the incidence of subsequent CPKP bacteremia and febrile neutropenia episodes.
proposals.method: We will construct a Markov state-transition model to simulate the clinical pathway of patients from pre-chemotherapy through potential infection outcomes. Model parameters for CPKP colonization prevalence, decolonization efficacy, and bacteremia risk will be derived from a systematic literature review of pre-2026 studies. Costs (probiotic regimen, antibiotic therapy, hospitalization) and utilities will be sourced from hospital databases and published literature. The analysis will be performed from a healthcare system perspective, calculating the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) against a willingness-to-pay threshold of $100,000 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). Deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses will assess model robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal economic evaluation of a microbiome stewardship intervention for MDRO prevention in neutropenia, directly informing hospital infection control policy. It will quantify the potential economic value of shifting from a treatment-focused to a prevention-focused paradigm, integrating clinical microbiology, ecology, and health economics.
proposals.novelty: While probiotics and decolonization have been studied separately, their synthesis into a pre-emptive, ecology-based strategy for CPKP in neutropenia is novel. Applying a rigorous health economic model to this specific microbial ecological intervention represents a significant methodological advance beyond typical clinical efficacy studies.

proposals.title: Clinical Utility of Karius Plasma Next-Generation Sequencing Test for Early Detection of Polymicrobial Infections in Non-Neutropenic Sepsis: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Sepsis in non-neutropenic patients, particularly in the ICU, carries high mortality, and timely, accurate pathogen identification is critical for appropriate antimicrobial therapy. Conventional blood cultures have limited sensitivity, especially for fastidious organisms, and often fail to detect polymicrobial infections, leading to delayed or inadequate treatment. The Karius Test (KT), a plasma next-generation sequencing assay, has shown high sensitivity for detecting a broad range of pathogens in immunocompromised hosts, but its value in a broader sepsis population is undefined.
proposals.hypothesis: The Karius Test will identify causative pathogens, particularly polymicrobial infections, with greater sensitivity than standard blood culture in non-neutropenic patients with sepsis or septic shock, and this earlier detection will correlate with more frequent and appropriate antimicrobial regimen adjustments.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of 300 adult non-neutropenic patients meeting Sepsis-3 criteria upon ICU admission. Paired blood samples for standard culture and KT will be drawn at enrollment. The primary analysis will compare the sensitivity and time-to-identification of KT versus culture. Secondary analyses will assess the rate of polymicrobial detection, the association of KT results with subsequent antimicrobial modifications (e.g., de-escalation, targeted therapy for resistant organisms), and clinical outcomes. Statistical analysis will use McNemar's test for paired proportions and multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial evidence on the real-world diagnostic performance and clinical impact of plasma metagenomic sequencing in general sepsis. It will define the prevalence and clinical significance of polymicrobial bacteremia detected by KT in this population. The findings could inform guidelines on the use of rapid NGS diagnostics to improve antimicrobial stewardship and patient outcomes in severe sepsis.
proposals.novelty: While KT has been studied in febrile neutropenia and immunocompromised hosts, this proposal systematically evaluates its utility in the larger, more heterogeneous population of non-neutropenic sepsis, where diagnostic uncertainty and polymicrobial infections are also major challenges. It integrates KT results with detailed antimicrobial prescription data to assess direct clinical impact.

proposals.title: Beyond Mortality: A Composite Endpoint of Infection-Specific Morbidity and Recurrence for Evaluating Antibiotic Efficacy in Gram-Negative Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: All-cause mortality, while definitive, is a blunt and often underpowered outcome for antibiotic trials, especially in immunocompromised hosts where competing risks of death are high. This can obscure clinically significant differences in infection-specific outcomes, such as persistent fever, microbiological failure, or relapse, which are critical to patient recovery and antimicrobial stewardship. A more nuanced endpoint is needed to better inform clinical decisions for high-risk infections.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite endpoint of infection-specific morbidity (e.g., persistent bacteremia, fever, or clinical instability) and microbiological recurrence within 30 days will be a more sensitive and clinically relevant measure of antibiotic failure than 30-day all-cause mortality in trials involving immunocompromised patients with Gram-negative bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of a retrospective cohort of immunocompromised patients with bacteremia due to organisms like Enterobacter cloacae, treated with piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or a carbapenem. Using multivariable logistic regression and competing risk models, we will compare the performance of the novel composite endpoint against all-cause mortality. The analysis will adjust for established risk factors (e.g., severity of illness, source control) and evaluate the endpoint's discriminant validity and association with key processes of care.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, infection-focused outcome measure that is more directly attributable to antimicrobial efficacy, potentially increasing the statistical power and clinical applicability of comparative effectiveness studies. It will challenge the reflexive use of all-cause mortality and offer a framework for designing future trials, particularly in populations where mortality is a poor proxy for antibiotic failure.
proposals.novelty: While composite endpoints exist, few are specifically designed for infectious diseases by integrating objective, infection-specific morbidity metrics with recurrence, moving beyond mortality or generic clinical failure. This directly addresses a gap highlighted by recent debates on antibiotic trial outcomes and leverages Deresinski's expertise in bacteremia outcomes and immunocompromised hosts.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Oral Step-Down Therapy for Bacteremia Caused by Extended-Spectrum Beta-Lactamase (ESBL) and AmpC-Producing Enterobacterales in Immunocompromised Patients: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The rise of antibiotic-resistant Gram-negative infections necessitates prolonged intravenous (IV) therapy, increasing hospital stays, costs, and line-associated complications. While oral step-down is a cornerstone of antimicrobial stewardship for susceptible infections, its safety and efficacy for resistant organisms like ESBL and AmpC producers remain poorly defined, especially in high-risk immunocompromised hosts. This gap leads to inconsistent clinical practice and potential for avoidable harm from either prolonged IV therapy or ineffective oral regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: In immunocompromised patients with bacteremia due to ESBL or AmpC-producing Enterobacterales, oral step-down therapy with highly bioavailable agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) after initial clinical response to IV therapy is non-inferior to continued IV therapy in terms of clinical cure and microbiological relapse, and is associated with reduced length of stay.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised adults (e.g., hematologic malignancy, solid organ transplant, high-dose corticosteroids) with bacteremia due to ESBL or AmpC-producing E. coli, Klebsiella spp., or Enterobacter cloacae complex from 2018-2024. Using electronic health records, we will compare patients who received oral step-down (exposed) to those maintained on IV therapy (control). Primary outcome is a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality or bacteremia recurrence. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders (e.g., source control, severity of illness, specific resistance mechanism). A non-inferiority margin will be pre-specified.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide urgently needed real-world evidence to guide stewardship and de-escalation practices for resistant Gram-negative bacteremia in vulnerable populations. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on the feasibility and optimal selection of oral step-down agents, potentially reducing healthcare utilization and improving patient quality of life without compromising outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Deresinski et al. compared initial IV regimens for non-susceptible organisms, this proposal uniquely focuses on the de-escalation phase to oral therapy, a critical stewardship decision point. It integrates the researcher's expertise in immunocompromised hosts, resistant infections, and comparative effectiveness of beta-lactams to address a subsequent, pragmatic clinical question.

proposals.title: Impact of Rapid Nanopore-Based mecA/C and SCCmec Typing on Time to Effective Therapy and Clinical Outcomes in Patients with MRSA Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) bacteremia is associated with high mortality, and delayed effective antimicrobial therapy worsens outcomes. Current diagnostic workflows, relying on culture and phenotypic susceptibility testing, result in a 48-72 hour delay in confirming MRSA and its resistance profile, leading to prolonged empirical vancomycin use. Rapid, point-of-care genomic identification of MRSA and its specific resistance determinants could enable earlier, targeted therapy, potentially improving patient outcomes and supporting antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a rapid, point-of-care Oxford Nanopore sequencing assay for direct detection of mecA/C genes and Staphylococcal Chromosomal Cassette mec (SCCmec) typing from positive blood culture bottles will significantly reduce time to effective, guideline-concordant therapy for MRSA bacteremia compared to standard microbiology workflows.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, quasi-experimental study in immunocompromised and other high-risk patients with S. aureus bacteremia. Positive blood culture broths will be processed in parallel: standard culture-based ID/AST versus a rapid nucleic acid extraction followed by multiplex PCR for S. aureus-specific markers (nuc, mecA/C) and SCCmec types I-V. Amplicons will be sequenced on a MinION Mk1C device, with basecalling and analysis performed in real-time using the EPI2ME wf-bacterial-multiplex workflow (Oxford Nanopore) for rapid variant calling and reporting. Clinical teams will be notified of results from the rapid assay, and time-to-therapy changes will be tracked.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical outcome data on the integration of real-time, point-of-care nanopore sequencing for MRSA resistance profiling directly from blood cultures. It will quantify the reduction in time to effective therapy and assess the feasibility of using SCCmec typing to inform early de-escalation or escalation decisions, moving beyond simple mecA detection to more nuanced resistance prediction.
proposals.novelty: While rapid PCR for mecA exists, this approach integrates rapid whole-amplicon sequencing to provide not just presence/absence of resistance genes but also SCCmec type, offering insights into associated beta-lactam resistance patterns (e.g., cefoxitin) and potential strain epidemiology at the point of care, a significant advance over static PCR panels.

proposals.title: Predicting Persistent MRSA Bacteremia from Host Immune Signatures: A Machine Learning Approach to an Inverse Clinical Problem
proposals.motivation: Persistent MRSA bacteremia is associated with high mortality and treatment failure, yet predicting which patients will develop this complication remains a major clinical challenge. Current risk models rely heavily on microbiological and clinical factors, but the host immune response is a critical, underutilized determinant of outcome. This study addresses the inverse problem of using early host signatures to predict a downstream microbiological failure, aligning with Deresinski's focus on infection risk in immunocompromised patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, early host immune response signatures, measurable in peripheral blood, can accurately predict which patients with initial MRSA bacteremia will progress to persistent infection despite appropriate antibiotic therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with MRSA bacteremia, stratified by outcome (persistent vs. resolved). Using pre-treatment serum, we will measure a broad panel of host immune markers (cytokines, chemokines, acute phase proteins) via Luminex multiplex assays. These high-dimensional data will be used to train and validate a predictive model. We will employ a Random Forest classifier for its robustness with high-dimensional data and ability to handle non-linear interactions, with feature selection via LASSO regression to identify the most predictive immune signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, host-focused predictive model for a serious infectious complication, moving beyond traditional clinical risk factors. It will identify specific immune pathways associated with treatment failure, offering potential targets for immunomodulatory adjunctive therapy and enabling earlier, more aggressive intervention in high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Deresinski and others has compared antibiotic efficacy for bacteremia, this proposal inverts the problem to use the host, not the bug or drug, as the primary predictor of failure. It integrates high-dimensional immunophenotyping with machine learning in a way not previously applied to predicting persistence in MRSA bacteremia.

proposals.title: Impact of Donor Gut Virome Modulation on Post-Transplant Bacteremia and Clinical Outcomes in Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: The recipient gut microbiome is a known determinant of infection risk and survival after hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT). However, the donor's contribution, particularly their virome (bacteriophages), remains unexplored despite its potential to shape the recipient's engrafting microbiota and resistome. Given the high mortality from multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacteremias in immunocompromised patients, a novel strategy targeting the donor ecosystem to protect the recipient is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: That pre-transplant modulation of the donor gut virome, using defined bacteriophage cocktails, will reduce the incidence of antibiotic-resistant bacteremia and improve overall survival in HCT recipients by favorably altering the engrafted gut microbiome.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, matched cohort study. Living donors will be screened and their gut virome characterized via metagenomic sequencing. A cohort of donors will receive an oral, defined bacteriophage cocktail targeting antibiotic-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (e.g., ESBL-producing E. cloacae, K. aerogenes) prior to donation. Recipients from modulated and unmodulated donors will be matched for clinical risk factors. The primary outcome is the incidence of bacteremia with cefoxitin-non-susceptible organisms in the first 100 days post-HCT. Secondary outcomes include microbiome engraftment dynamics (analyzed using tools like MetaPhlAn and HUMAnN) and recipient mortality. Quantitative microbial risk assessment frameworks will be adapted to model the intervention's effect on bacteremia risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept that the donor virome is a modifiable factor influencing recipient infectious outcomes. It will provide the first clinical data on targeted virome manipulation as a preventive strategy against antibiotic-resistant infections in HCT. The findings could redefine pre-transplant donor preparation protocols.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the paradigm of focusing solely on the recipient's microbiota post-transplant by proactively engineering the donor's microbial ecosystem. It uniquely applies bacteriophage therapy not as a treatment for an established infection, but as a pre-emptive, ecological intervention in a donor to protect an immunocompromised host.

proposals.title: Differential Antiviral Effects of Immunosuppressive Regimens: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Emerging Viral Infections in Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: The paradigm in infectious diseases assumes immunosuppression uniformly increases infection risk. However, emerging clinical observations and basic science suggest certain immunosuppressive agents may have direct or indirect antiviral properties. This is critically important for managing transplant and immunocompromised patients facing threats from emerging viruses like SARS-CoV-2, Monkeypox virus (MPXV), and severe arboviruses, where optimal immunosuppression management could mitigate infection risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific immunosuppressive regimens (e.g., mTOR inhibitors like sirolimus, calcineurin inhibitors, mycophenolate) differentially modulate the risk and clinical severity of infection with specific emerging viruses, with some agents conferring a net protective effect against certain viral pathogens.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients from 2020-2025. The primary exposure will be the immunosuppressive regimen at the time of index testing. Outcomes will include laboratory-confirmed infection with pre-specified emerging viruses (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, MPXV, Dengue, Chikungunya) and associated severe disease. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders (e.g., transplant type, time post-transplant, lymphocyte count). A parallel in vitro analysis will assess viral replication kinetics in primary cells treated with these agents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale clinical evidence that immunosuppression is not monolithic in its effect on viral infection risk. It will generate a clinically actionable 'risk profile' of common regimens against specific viruses, informing preventive strategies and empirical management in immunocompromised hosts during outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the foundational clinical assumption that all immunosuppression is permissive for all infections, leveraging the natural experiment of emerging virus outbreaks to test for selective antiviral effects of drugs used for other purposes.

proposals.title: Integrating Global Mobility Networks with Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment to Predict the Dissolution of Geographic Risk Boundaries for Respiratory Viruses
proposals.motivation: Traditional infectious disease risk models rely heavily on static geographic boundaries, which are increasingly obsolete in a hyper-connected world. Pre-2026 events like the global spread of SARS-CoV-2 variants and Mpox demonstrated how air travel and migration rapidly dissolve these boundaries, creating unpredictable risks for immunocompromised patients and straining clinical preparedness. A dynamic, mobility-informed framework is urgently needed to move from reactive to proactive risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating anonymized, real-time global air travel and human mobility data with established Quantitative Microbial Risk Assessment (QMRA) frameworks will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting the introduction and establishment risk of specific respiratory viruses (e.g., influenza, endemic coronaviruses, RSV) into previously low-risk geographic regions, outperforming models based on historical incidence alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model where nodes represent major global population centers. Layers will incorporate: 1) anonymized commercial air passenger volume data (IATA/OpenSky Network), 2) anonymized human mobility estimates (Meta Data for Good, Google COVID-19 Mobility Reports), and 3) pre-2026 regional virological surveillance data (e.g., WHO FluNet, CDC). This network will be coupled with a modified QMRA framework, adapted from the PI's prior aerosol tracer work, to simulate pathogen importation probability and early community transmission risk. Model validation will use retrospective case data from 2015-2019 for specific virus introductions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source modeling framework that quantifies how mobility dissolves viral risk boundaries, providing a tool for hospitals to anticipate pathogen introductions. It will directly inform clinical infection prevention strategies for immunocompromised patients and bridge the gap between the PI's expertise in clinical outcomes (e.g., bacteremia) and population-level transmission dynamics. The results will offer actionable data for 'pre-emptive' rather than reactive infection control.
proposals.novelty: While mobility data has been used in epidemiological models, its integration into a formal QMRA framework—tailored for hospital and immunocompromised patient risk—is novel. This approach moves beyond describing spread to quantifying the changing infection risk for specific vulnerable populations based on dynamic global connectivity, a direct extension of the PI's work on integrating aerosol tracers with QMRA for building safety.

proposals.title: Unbiased Metagenomic Next-Generation Sequencing for Comprehensive Viral Pathogen Detection and Risk Stratification in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) patients are at high risk for severe viral infections, which are a major cause of morbidity and mortality. Current diagnostic methods are often targeted, slow, and can miss novel or unexpected pathogens. An unbiased, comprehensive diagnostic approach is urgently needed to improve early detection and guide preemptive therapy, thereby reducing infection-related complications in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying unbiased metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) to plasma from febrile HSCT patients will identify a broader spectrum of viral pathogens, including those missed by standard tests, and the detection of specific viruses or viral communities will be associated with distinct clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will perform shotgun mNGS on serial plasma samples from a prospective cohort of HSCT patients with febrile episodes. Nucleic acids will be extracted, and libraries prepared for sequencing on an Illumina platform. Bioinformatic analysis will utilize established, pre-2026 pipelines (e.g., SURPI, Kraken2) for taxonomic classification against comprehensive viral databases (NCBI RefSeq). Clinical metadata and outcomes will be integrated for risk assessment.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the diagnostic yield and clinical utility of plasma mNGS for viral detection in HSCT patients. It will generate data on the prevalence of unexpected viral infections and identify viral signatures associated with adverse outcomes, informing future surveillance and preemptive treatment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While mNGS is established in environmental ecology, its systematic application for unbiased viral surveillance in the dynamic clinical setting of HSCT, integrated with quantitative risk assessment, represents a novel translation of ecological methods to a high-stakes patient population.

proposals.title: Network-Based Modeling of Nosocomial and Donor-Derived Viral Transmission Pathways to Inform Infection Prevention and Risk Stratification
proposals.motivation: Viral infections, including respiratory viruses (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, influenza) and donor-derived threats (e.g., CMV, HCV), pose significant risks to immunocompromised patients in healthcare settings. Current infection control practices often rely on static risk factors and lack dynamic tools to predict transmission chains, limiting proactive intervention. Integrating network theory with clinical and microbiological data offers a powerful framework to model these pathways and quantify transmission risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying epidemiological network models to integrated patient, pathogen, and healthcare contact data will accurately reconstruct and predict nosocomial and donor-recipient viral transmission chains, identifying key nodes and edges that disproportionately drive infection risk.
proposals.method: We will construct temporal, bipartite networks linking patients, donors, healthcare workers, and locations using electronic health record data. Nodes represent individuals or locations, and edges represent contacts (e.g., shared rooms, procedures, donor organs). We will integrate viral genomic sequencing data to validate inferred transmission links. Models will be implemented using stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) in R/RSiena and agent-based simulations to test intervention scenarios, such as targeted screening or isolation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven tool for hospital epidemiology to visualize and interrupt viral transmission networks. It will generate specific, testable strategies for reducing nosocomial and transplant-related viral infections, directly extending Dr. Deresinski's research on risk assessment and antimicrobial use into the viral domain. The framework will also bridge clinical virology with quantitative microbial risk assessment methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While network models are used in community epidemiology, their application to integrate donor-derived transmission with nosocomial spread in real-world hospital data is novel. This approach moves beyond bacterial/fungal focus to model complex viral transmission, leveraging pre-2026 genomic and contact-tracing technologies within a unified network theory framework.

proposals.title: Quantitative Risk-Benefit Modeling of Immunosuppression Regimens to Minimize Composite Risk of Graft Rejection and Viral Infection in Solid Organ Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Solid organ transplant recipients require lifelong immunosuppression to prevent graft rejection, but this simultaneously elevates the risk of opportunistic viral infections, particularly with cytomegalovirus (CMV), BK polyomavirus, and Epstein-Barr virus. Current clinical practice relies on qualitative guidelines and therapeutic drug monitoring, lacking a quantitative framework to model the dynamic trade-off between rejection and infection risk under different immunosuppressive drug exposures. This gap is critical for optimizing patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacodynamic model integrating quantitative measures of immunosuppression intensity (e.g., trough levels, lymphocyte subset counts) with viral load kinetics can identify regimen-specific 'sweet spots' that minimize the composite risk of acute cellular rejection and clinically significant viral infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult kidney and liver transplant recipients from 2015-2024, extracting detailed data on immunosuppressive drug levels, lymphocyte profiles, biopsy-proven rejection, and viral PCR loads. We will employ a time-varying Cox proportional hazards model with competing risks to quantify the association between time-updated immunosuppression intensity and the hazards of rejection and viral infection. A composite risk score will be developed using machine learning (XGBoost) to predict the probability of an adverse event (rejection or viremia >10^4 copies/mL) over a 90-day horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, data-driven model to visualize the dual-risk landscape of immunosuppression, offering clinicians a tool to personalize dosing strategies. It will generate testable hypotheses about optimal target ranges for immunosuppressive drugs in the context of specific viral infection risks, directly informing future interventional trials.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined infection or rejection risks separately, this study integrates them into a single competing-risk modeling framework, leveraging detailed longitudinal clinical and microbiological data specific to transplant patients, a population central to Dr. Deresinski's research themes.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial of a Targeted Gut Decolonization Protocol to Prevent Carbapenemase-Producing Klebsiella pneumoniae (CPKP) Bacteremia in High-Risk Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly CPKP, cause high-mortality infections, especially in immunocompromised patients. Current research heavily focuses on optimizing antibiotic treatment for active bacteremia, but this is a reactive strategy. A proactive strategy to prevent colonization, the critical precursor to invasive infection, could significantly reduce morbidity and mortality in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: A protocol combining selective digestive decontamination (SDD) and a defined course of oral, non-absorbable antibiotics (e.g., gentamicin/colistin) will effectively eradicate gut colonization by CPKP in high-risk hematology/oncology patients, leading to a significant reduction in the subsequent incidence of CPKP bacteremia compared to standard infection control measures alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized, open-label trial. Immunocompromised patients (e.g., with hematologic malignancies, neutropenia) identified as CPKP rectal carriers via active surveillance will be randomized to intervention or control. The intervention arm will receive a 7-day course of oral, non-absorbable gentamicin and colistin, plus chlorhexidine mouthwash. The primary endpoint will be the incidence of CPKP bacteremia within 60 days. Secondary endpoints include microbiological decolonization success (serial rectal swabs), safety, and impact on gut microbiome diversity (assessed via 16S rRNA sequencing on pre- and post-treatment stool samples).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level clinical evidence for a targeted decolonization strategy against CPKP in a high-risk immunocompromised cohort. It will define the feasibility, efficacy, and potential ecological consequences of such an intervention, directly informing infection prevention protocols and potentially shifting the standard of care from reactive treatment to proactive prevention for CRE.
proposals.novelty: While SDD is studied in ICU settings, its targeted application specifically for CPKP in non-ICU, immunocompromised patients is novel. This proposal integrates Deresinski's expertise in bacteremia outcomes and immunocompromised hosts with a preventive, microbiologically-focused intervention, inverting the traditional focus from treatment failure to colonization blockade.

proposals.title: Prospective, Multicenter Study of Herpesvirus Reactivation and Clinical Outcomes to Define Antiviral Prophylaxis Need in Patients Receiving Natalizumab or Fingolimod
proposals.motivation: Natalizumab and fingolimod are immunomodulatory therapies for multiple sclerosis (MS) associated with increased risk of opportunistic infections, including herpesviruses (e.g., HSV, VZV). Current guidelines lack specific, evidence-based recommendations for antiviral prophylaxis in this population, leading to inconsistent clinical practice and potential preventable morbidity. A prospective study is needed to quantify the incidence and severity of herpesvirus events to inform a standardized risk-stratified approach.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients receiving natalizumab or fingolimod have a clinically significant incidence of symptomatic herpesvirus reactivation (e.g., herpes zoster, mucocutaneous HSV) that justifies targeted antiviral prophylaxis. The risk profile differs between these agents and is influenced by prior serostatus and treatment duration.
proposals.method: We propose a 24-month, prospective, observational cohort study across three academic medical centers. Adult MS patients initiating or continuing natalizumab or fingolimod will be enrolled. The primary endpoint is the incidence of clinically significant herpesvirus infection (confirmed by PCR or culture). Secondary endpoints include severity (hospitalization, disseminated disease), viral load kinetics via serial quantitative PCR in a subset, and identification of risk factors (e.g., age, lymphocyte subsets, prior herpes history). Statistical analysis will employ time-to-event methods (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards) to compare incidence between drug cohorts and against a historical control cohort of MS patients on non-immunosuppressive therapies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, multicenter data on the absolute risk of herpesvirus complications with these specific agents, directly addressing a current evidence gap in neuro-infectious disease. The results will enable the development of a data-driven clinical decision tool to guide prophylaxis, potentially reducing infection-related morbidity and healthcare utilization. It will also establish a framework for monitoring viral reactivation in immunomodulated patients.
proposals.novelty: While infection risks of these drugs are known, no study has prospectively and systematically quantified herpesvirus-specific reactivation rates with parallel virological monitoring to definitively assess prophylaxis need. This integrates Deresinski's expertise in clinical and microbiological risk assessment into the neuro-immunology space, applying a methodology used in transplant/oncology to a distinct patient population.

proposals.title: Comparative Risk of Hepatitis B Virus Reactivation Associated with Oral Disease-Modifying Therapies in Multiple Sclerosis: A Multicenter Cohort Study and Systematic Review
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the risk of hepatitis B virus (HBV) reactivation is well-established for B-cell depleting monoclonal antibodies (e.g., ocrelizumab, rituximab) used in multiple sclerosis (MS). However, the risk profile for the expanding class of oral small-molecule agents (e.g., fingolimod, siponimod, ozanimod, ponesimod, teriflunomide, dimethyl fumarate) remains poorly characterized and inconsistently stratified. This knowledge gap creates clinical uncertainty in screening, monitoring, and prophylactic strategies for a large patient population with MS.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oral MS agents with specific mechanisms of immune modulation, particularly sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulators, confer a quantifiable and variable risk of HBV reactivation in patients with resolved or active infection, distinct from the high risk associated with anti-CD20 therapies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of MS patients receiving oral disease-modifying therapies with available HBV serology (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs). The primary outcome is HBV reactivation, defined as a confirmed increase in HBV DNA or seroreversion. We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to estimate hazard ratios, adjusting for baseline serostatus, age, and concomitant immunosuppression. This will be integrated with a systematic review and meta-analysis of published cases and cohorts to generate pooled risk estimates for each drug class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, evidence-based stratification of HBV reactivation risk across all oral MS therapies, directly informing pre-treatment screening guidelines and monitoring protocols. It will clarify whether universal antiviral prophylaxis is necessary for certain drug classes or if risk is sufficiently low to recommend monitoring alone, thereby optimizing patient safety and resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: While HBV reactivation risk is characterized for biologics, a systematic, comparative assessment focusing exclusively on the heterogeneous class of oral small-molecule MS drugs, which have diverse and often less immunosuppressive mechanisms, has not been performed. This study integrates detailed clinical and microbiological data analysis, a core strength of Dr. Deresinski's prior work on infection risk in immunocompromised patients.

proposals.title: Validation and Adaptation of Solid Organ Transplant Infection Risk Models for Risk Stratification in Multiple Sclerosis Patients Receiving High-Potency Immunosuppressive Therapies
proposals.motivation: Patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) on high-potency immunosuppressants (e.g., anti-CD20 agents, sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulators) face significant infection risks, but lack validated, quantitative tools for personalized risk assessment. In solid organ transplant (SOT) recipients, validated infection risk scores (e.g., based on age, lymphocyte count, prior infections, co-morbidities) are used to guide prophylaxis and monitoring. Applying this established SOT framework to MS could improve clinical management and pre-emptively identify high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: Infection risk stratification models validated in SOT populations can be adapted and recalibrated to accurately predict serious bacterial, fungal, and viral infections in MS patients undergoing long-term immunosuppressive therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of MS patients on immunosuppressants at Stanford (2015-2025). Primary outcome is serious infection (bacteremia, invasive fungal disease, hospitalization-requiring pneumonia). We will externally apply pre-2026 SOT risk models (e.g., the 'INCREMENT' or specific bacteremia risk scores) to this cohort. Model performance will be assessed using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and calibration plots. We will then refine the models using machine learning techniques (logistic regression with LASSO regularization) by incorporating MS-specific covariates (e.g., EDSS score, specific drug class, prior relapse history) to optimize predictive accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, clinically actionable infection risk score for MS patients on immunosuppressants, filling a critical gap in neuro-immunology practice. It will establish a framework for translating risk assessment paradigms across different immunosuppressed populations, potentially leading to targeted antimicrobial prophylaxis and reduced infection-related morbidity.
proposals.novelty: While infection risk modeling is mature in transplant, its direct application to the chronic, non-transplant immunosuppression context of MS is novel. The integration of MS-specific disability and treatment variables with transplant-derived microbiological risk factors represents a new, cross-disciplinary approach to patient safety.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Observational Study to Derive a Risk-Stratified Protocol for Hepatitis B Virus Reactivation in Multiple Sclerosis Patients Receiving B-Cell Depleting Therapies
proposals.motivation: B-cell depleting therapies (e.g., ocrelizumab, rituximab) for multiple sclerosis (MS) carry a significant risk for Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) reactivation, but standardized, evidence-based monitoring and prophylaxis guidelines are lacking. This creates clinical uncertainty and potential for preventable, severe hepatitis. In contrast, oncology has well-established, risk-stratified protocols for HBV management during chemotherapy, providing a proven framework for adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a risk-stratified management algorithm, adapted from oncology guidelines and based on baseline HBV serology (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs), will effectively identify MS patients at high risk for reactivation and allow for safe, targeted antiviral prophylaxis during B-cell depletion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective observational cohort study of MS patients initiating B-cell depleting therapy. Patients will be stratified at baseline into risk categories (high, moderate, low) using an adapted oncology algorithm. All patients will undergo protocolized serial monitoring of HBV DNA and liver function tests. The primary outcome is the incidence of HBV reactivation (defined as HBV DNA >100 IU/mL in previously inactive carriers or reverse seroconversion). Secondary outcomes include hepatitis flare rates and the predictive value of the risk categories. Statistical analysis will use time-to-event methods.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective data to validate a structured, serology-based risk algorithm for HBV reactivation in neuroimmunology. It will generate evidence to support standardized monitoring schedules and clear indications for antiviral prophylaxis, filling a critical gap in the management of MS patients on high-risk immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While the risk of HBV reactivation is recognized, this work systematically translates a successful management paradigm from oncology to neuroimmunology, moving beyond generic caution to a data-driven, risk-adapted protocol specific to the MS treatment population.

proposals.title: Quantitative Assessment of Herpesvirus-Specific T-Cell Clonotypes via Immune Repertoire Sequencing to Predict Reactivation Risk in Alemtuzumab-Treated Rheumatology Patients
proposals.motivation: Alemtuzumab, a potent anti-CD52 monoclonal antibody used in rheumatology and multiple sclerosis, induces profound and prolonged lymphopenia, significantly increasing the risk of life-threatening herpesvirus (e.g., CMV, VZV, HSV) reactivation. Current risk stratification relies on crude clinical factors and non-specific lymphocyte counts, failing to capture the functional integrity of virus-specific immunity. A pre-emptive, personalized microbiological risk assessment tool is urgently needed to guide prophylactic strategies and improve patient safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput T-cell receptor beta-chain (TCRβ) sequencing of peripheral blood, combined with bioinformatic inference of herpesvirus-specific clonotypes, can quantify the pre-existing repertoire of protective antiviral T-cells and predict the risk of clinical herpesvirus reactivation following alemtuzumab therapy.
proposals.method: We will perform bulk TCRβ sequencing (using the Adaptive Biotechnologies ImmunoSEQ platform) on peripheral blood mononuclear cells from rheumatology patients prior to alemtuzumab initiation. Using pre-2026 published algorithms (e.g., GLIPH2 for clustering TCRs with shared specificity and TCRdist for structural similarity), we will bioinformatically annotate and quantify clonotypes associated with common herpesviruses by matching against reference databases of viral epitope-specific TCRs. A multivariable Cox proportional hazards model will integrate these quantitative immune repertoire metrics (e.g., clonotype richness, Shannon diversity, and estimated virus-specific clone frequency) with clinical variables to predict time-to-reactivating infection.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel, sequence-based biomarker for personalized infectious risk assessment in immunocompromised patients, directly linking Deresinski's themes of clinical infection risk and microbiological precision. It will provide a framework for pre-emptive monitoring and targeted prophylaxis, moving beyond empirical management. The data will also offer fundamental insights into the architecture of protective antiviral immunity in autoimmune patients.
proposals.novelty: While immune repertoire sequencing has been used descriptively in oncology, its application as a predictive clinical microbiology tool for infection risk in non-transplant, drug-induced immunosuppression is novel. This integrates high-resolution immunogenomics with quantitative microbial risk assessment, a conceptual synergy highlighted in the researcher's recent work on aerosol transmission.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Personalized Infection Risk Dashboard Integrating Host Immune Profiling, Microbiological Data, and Antimicrobial Exposure for Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: Immunocompromised patients face heterogeneous and dynamic risks for infection, yet clinical decisions often rely on static risk scores. Recent work, including Deresinski's, highlights the critical interplay between specific antimicrobial exposures (e.g., piperacillin/tazobactam vs. cefepime), host factors like anticytokine autoantibodies, and microbiological data in determining infection outcomes. A tool that dynamically integrates these multidimensional data at the point of care is urgently needed to personalize prophylaxis and empiric therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A real-time, personalized dashboard that algorithmically integrates a patient's antimicrobial exposure history, vaccination status, latent infection markers (e.g., fungal serology, CMV PCR), and host immune profile (e.g., autoantibody screens) will more accurately predict subsequent infection risk and guide optimal antimicrobial selection compared to standard clinical assessment.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype dashboard using a modular, rules-based engine informed by pre-2026 clinical evidence. Inputs will include real-time EHR data (active antibiotics, dose, duration), historical vaccination records, and results from defined immune assays (e.g., anti-IFN-γ, anti-GM-CSF). A risk-scoring algorithm, built on a foundation of quantitative microbial risk assessment principles, will weight these factors using coefficients derived from Deresinski's prior bacteremia studies and the fungal autoantibody literature. The dashboard will output a visualized risk estimate for specific pathogens (e.g., ESBL Enterobacterales, invasive fungi) and a tiered management suggestion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for data-integrated, personalized infection risk assessment at the bedside. It will generate a testable framework for reducing inappropriate broad-spectrum antibiotic use and pre-emptively identifying patients at highest risk for specific opportunistic infections, directly extending the team's research on antibiotic failure and host defense.
proposals.novelty: While risk scores exist, this approach uniquely fuses real-time pharmacodynamic data (specific antibiotic regimens) with emerging host immune biomarkers and latent infection status into a single actionable output, moving beyond population-level epidemiology to individual dynamic prediction.

proposals.title: Infections as Biomarkers of Immunosuppression: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Infection Risk and Pathogen Profile in Patients on Disease-Modifying Therapies for Multiple Sclerosis
proposals.motivation: Disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) for multiple sclerosis (MS) modulate the immune system with varying intensity, directly impacting infection risk. Current assessments of DMT efficacy and immunosuppressive burden rely on clinical relapse rates, MRI lesions, and lymphocyte counts, but these may not fully capture functional immunocompromise. Specific infections could serve as direct, clinically relevant biomarkers of the degree and nature of immunosuppression induced by different MS therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: The incidence, timing, and microbiological etiology of serious infections (e.g., bacteremia, invasive fungal disease) in MS patients are correlated with the specific mechanism and intended immunosuppressive potency of their DMT, providing a real-world measure of therapeutic immunologic impact.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult MS patients initiating high-efficacy DMTs (e.g., anti-CD20 agents, sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulators, natalizumab) from 2015-2024. The primary outcome is time to first serious infection requiring hospitalization. Data will include infection type, causative pathogen (from microbiological records), and therapy duration. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for age, comorbidities, and prior steroid use, to compare infection risk between DMT classes. Pathogen-specific analyses (e.g., encapsulated bacteria vs. herpesviruses vs. fungi) will be mapped to known immune deficits.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish an infection-based framework for stratifying the real-world immunosuppressive risk of MS DMTs, moving beyond clinical trial data. It will provide clinicians with pathogen-specific risk profiles to guide monitoring and prophylaxis. The findings could inform a novel paradigm where infection patterns are used to gauge individual patient-level immunologic response to therapy.
proposals.novelty: While infection risk is known for many DMTs, this study systematically links specific microbiological etiologies to specific drug mechanisms as biomarkers of efficacy/immunosuppression, building on Deresinski's work in immunocompromised hosts and anticytokine autoantibodies. It applies a clinical infectious diseases lens to the neuroimmunology treatment landscape.

proposals.title: A Clinical Decision Support Algorithm for Multiple Sclerosis Therapy Selection Based on Prior Infection History and Serological Risk Profiling
proposals.motivation: Patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) on immunomodulatory therapies face an elevated risk of serious infections, including opportunistic fungal infections and bacteremia. Current therapy selection relies heavily on disease activity, with limited systematic integration of individual infection risk. Deresinski's prior work on anticytokine autoantibodies and infection risk in immunocompromised hosts provides a foundation for a more personalized risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning algorithm trained on historical clinical, microbiological, and serological data can identify patients at high risk for specific infections and recommend the safest MS therapy, minimizing infection-related morbidity without compromising disease control.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort using electronic health records from MS patients, capturing infection history, serology (e.g., anticytokine autoantibodies, immunoglobulin levels, vaccination titers), and MS therapy sequences. A supervised learning model, such as a gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost) or a recurrent neural network (RNN) for temporal sequences, will be trained to predict post-therapy infection events. The model's output will be adapted into a decision-support tool that ranks available MS therapies by predicted infection risk for a given patient profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, data-driven framework to personalize MS therapy selection, directly addressing infection risk mitigation. It will translate complex microbiological and immunological risk factors, as studied in Deresinski's prior research, into actionable clinical guidance, potentially reducing treatment failure and severe infections.
proposals.novelty: While infection risk is known, the inverse engineering of therapy selection based on a comprehensive infection and serology history represents a novel, patient-centric application of machine learning in neuro-immunology, moving beyond disease activity as the sole decision driver.

proposals.title: Direct Invasion Without Detectable Colonization: A Prospective Study of Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus (VRE) Bacteremia in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 clinical practice, infection prevention for VRE in high-risk HSCT recipients relies heavily on surveillance cultures and isolation of colonized patients, based on the paradigm that intestinal colonization is a necessary precursor to invasive disease. This strategy is resource-intensive and has significant patient quality-of-life implications. If a meaningful proportion of VRE bacteremias originate from sources other than established, detectable colonization, current prevention protocols may be insufficiently protective and misdirected.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of VRE bacteremia episodes in HSCT recipients occurs in patients with no prior rectal or stool culture evidence of VRE colonization, suggesting direct mucosal translocation or hematogenous seeding from an occult source.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of adult HSCT recipients at a major transplant center. All patients will undergo standardized weekly rectal swab surveillance for VRE via selective culture from admission through day 100 post-transplant. For all patients who develop VRE bacteremia, we will analyze the preceding surveillance results. The primary outcome is the proportion of bacteremia episodes in patients with no positive surveillance culture in the 4 weeks prior to the first positive blood culture. All VRE blood and surveillance isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing (Illumina platform) and core-genome multilocus sequence typing (cgMLST) to assess clonality and rule out lab error or contamination.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, prospective estimate of the frequency of 'non-colonized' VRE bacteremia in HSCT recipients, directly challenging a foundational assumption of infection control. The genomic analysis will clarify whether these episodes represent undetected low-level colonization or truly novel acquisition leading to rapid invasion. The findings will inform the evidence base for targeted versus universal screening and isolation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior retrospective studies note VRE bacteremia in culture-negative patients, this is the first designed to prospectively and rigorously test the colonization-precedes-infection axiom using frequent, standardized surveillance coupled with genomic confirmation, a method not yet applied to this specific clinical question.

proposals.title: Genomic Determinants of Virulence: Association of Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecium Lineages with Invasive Disease Risk in Immunocompromised Hosts
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE), particularly E. faecium, is a leading cause of healthcare-associated bacteremia, with high mortality in immunocompromised patients. Current clinical paradigms and infection prevention strategies often treat VRE as a monolith, assuming uniform virulence. However, pre-2026 genomic epidemiology suggests the existence of high-risk clonal complexes (e.g., CC17), but a direct, quantitative link between specific genetic lineages and the risk of progression from colonization to invasive disease in vulnerable hosts remains poorly defined, hindering targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific VRE faecium genetic lineages and their associated genomic repertoires (e.g., virulence factors, resistance determinants, metabolic adaptations) confer a significantly higher risk of causing invasive bloodstream infection compared to colonization in immunocompromised patient populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised patients (e.g., hematologic malignancy, transplant) with prior VRE rectal colonization. Cases will be patients who develop subsequent VRE bacteremia; controls will be colonized patients who do not. All archived VRE isolates (both colonizing and bacteremia) will undergo whole-genome sequencing. We will use core genome multilocus sequence typing (cgMLST) and phylogenetic analysis to define lineages. Association of lineage, specific sequence types (STs), and the presence of virulence genes (e.g., esp, hyl, sgrA) with invasive disease will be assessed using multivariate logistic regression, controlling for clinical risk factors (neutropenia, ICU stay, antibiotic exposure).
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond simple species-level resistance profiling by identifying high-risk VRE clones, enabling more precise risk stratification for immunocompromised patients. The findings could inform targeted infection prevention interventions (e.g., enhanced isolation for carriers of high-risk strains) and guide the development of rapid molecular diagnostics to detect these virulent lineages directly from clinical samples.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe VRE population structure, this proposal directly links detailed genomic data with meticulously defined clinical outcomes (invasive disease vs. colonization) in a high-risk immunocompromised cohort, testing the core assumption of uniform virulence that underpins many infection control policies.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Infection Control: A Multicenter, Stepwise Intervention Study to Isolate the Impact of Contact Precautions on Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus Transmission in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Units
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) colonization and infection remain a significant cause of morbidity and mortality in immunocompromised patients, particularly those undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT). While infection control bundles for VRE are widely implemented, their individual components—especially resource-intensive strict contact isolation—have not been rigorously deconstructed to assess their independent efficacy in the modern HSCT care environment. Determining the most effective and efficient strategies is critical for patient safety and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: In modern HSCT units with high baseline adherence to hand hygiene and environmental cleaning, the removal of strict contact precautions (gowns and gloves) for VRE-colonized patients will not lead to a significant increase in hospital-acquired VRE transmission rates compared to periods when full contact precautions are enforced.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, prospective, stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial across 8-10 high-volume HSCT centers. All units will implement a core bundle of hand hygiene promotion and enhanced environmental disinfection. Units will be randomized to sequentially cross over from a phase of "full precautions" (contact isolation plus core bundle) to "modified precautions" (core bundle only, with gloves recommended for direct contact with bodily fluids but no gowns or dedicated equipment). The primary outcome will be the incidence density of hospital-acquired VRE colonization, measured via weekly perirectal surveillance cultures in all patients. Transmission will be modeled using multivariable Poisson regression, adjusting for cluster and time effects, with microbial genotyping (e.g., pulsed-field gel electrophoresis) used to confirm relatedness of isolates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to either validate or challenge the central role of contact isolation in VRE prevention for this vulnerable population. It will directly inform national infection control guidelines by quantifying the marginal benefit of this costly intervention. The integrated use of clinical epidemiology, microbiological surveillance, and molecular typing aligns with Dr. Deresinski's expertise in bridging clinical risk and microbiological outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have assessed VRE bundles, this is the first designed to prospectively isolate the effect of contact precautions in a randomized design within the unique, high-risk HSCT setting, moving beyond observational data to a causal test of a foundational infection control tenet.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial Comparing Oral Linezolid, Oral Daptomycin, and a Novel Oral Ramoplanin Regimen for Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus (VRE) Decolonization in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant (HSCT) Recipients
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) colonization is a major risk factor for subsequent VRE bacteremia in HSCT patients, leading to increased mortality, prolonged hospitalization, and high healthcare costs. Despite this, there is no consensus on an optimal decolonization strategy, with existing studies showing variable efficacy of agents like oral linezolid and daptomycin. Defining a standardized, effective regimen is a critical unmet need in this high-risk, immunocompromised population.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel oral ramoplanin-based decolonization regimen will achieve superior rates of sustained VRE eradication from the gastrointestinal tract in HSCT patients compared to standard oral linezolid or oral daptomycin, and this superior decolonization will translate to a significant reduction in the incidence of VRE bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label, three-arm trial at a major transplant center. Adult patients undergoing allogeneic HSCT found to be colonized with VRE via rectal swab PCR will be randomized to receive: 1) oral linezolid 600 mg twice daily for 14 days, 2) oral daptomycin (liquid formulation) 200 mg twice daily for 14 days, or 3) oral ramoplanin 200 mg twice daily for 14 days. The primary endpoint will be successful decolonization, defined as negative serial rectal swab cultures at the end of therapy and at 30 days post-HSCT engraftment. Secondary endpoints will include incidence of VRE bacteremia up to 100 days post-transplant, time to bacteremia, and assessment of adverse events and microbiome disruption via 16S rRNA sequencing on stool samples.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first head-to-head comparative efficacy data for the three most promising oral VRE decolonization agents in the HSCT setting. It will establish a clinical benchmark for decolonization success and directly inform evidence-based infection prevention guidelines. Furthermore, it will generate crucial safety and microbiome impact data to guide the selection of the optimal agent.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined single agents, this is the first controlled trial to directly compare linezolid, daptomycin, and ramoplanin, and the first to evaluate the clinical use of oral ramoplanin—a non-absorbed lipoglycodepsipeptide with potent in vitro activity against VRE—specifically for decolonization in this population.
