proposals.title: Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus Colonization and its Independent Association with Mortality and Graft-versus-Host Disease in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) colonization is highly prevalent in immunocompromised patients, particularly those undergoing hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT). While VRE bacteremia is a known complication, the independent impact of pre-transplant colonization on key outcomes like overall mortality and graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), separate from the risk of subsequent infection, remains poorly defined and controversial. Clarifying this relationship is critical for informing infection prevention strategies and risk stratification in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-transplant VRE colonization is an independent risk factor for increased non-relapse mortality and the development of moderate-to-severe acute graft-versus-host disease in allogeneic HCT recipients, even after adjusting for subsequent VRE bacteremia and other established clinical confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult allogeneic HCT recipients from 2015-2024. The primary exposure is pre-transplant VRE colonization, defined by a positive rectal swab PCR or culture within 30 days prior to transplant. The primary outcomes are 1-year non-relapse mortality and grade II-IV acute GVHD. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression and competing risks regression to assess the independent association of colonization with outcomes, adjusting for key confounders including age, underlying disease, conditioning intensity, donor type, subsequent VRE bacteremia, and antibiotic exposure (e.g., anti-pseudomonal beta-lactams like piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime). Propensity score matching will be used to validate findings in a balanced cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, multicenter evidence on whether VRE colonization itself is a clinically significant risk factor for poor transplant outcomes, beyond its role as a precursor to infection. It will directly inform debates on the utility of pre-transplant screening and the need for colonization-targeted interventions. The findings will add crucial data to the field of transplant infectious diseases by integrating microbiological colonization status with hard clinical endpoints.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies often conflate colonization with subsequent infection, this analysis rigorously isolates the independent effect of the colonized state on GVHD and mortality, a distinction not fully elucidated in pre-2026 literature. Furthermore, it integrates detailed antibiotic exposure data to control for a key potential confounder and modifier of the gut microbiome.

proposals.title: A Prospective Evaluation of a Multiplex PCR and Nanopore Sequencing-Based Assay for Rapid Detection of Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus (VRE) Colonization and Bacteremia in Immunocompromised Hosts
proposals.motivation: VRE colonization is a critical precursor to invasive infection, particularly in immunocompromised patients, and delays in detection lead to delayed isolation and suboptimal empiric therapy. Current culture-based methods for detecting VRE colonization or confirming VRE bacteremia require 24-72 hours, creating a window of risk for transmission and clinical failure. Rapid, accurate diagnostics are urgently needed to inform timely infection control measures and targeted antimicrobial therapy in high-risk populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A diagnostic workflow combining rectal swab enrichment broth, multiplex PCR for *vanA/vanB* genes, and rapid nanopore sequencing for species confirmation and strain typing will significantly reduce the time to detection of VRE colonization and bacteremia compared to standard culture, without compromising specificity.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll immunocompromised patients at high risk for VRE. For colonization, rectal swabs will be processed in parallel: standard culture and in a novel broth enriched with aztreonam and ceftriaxone to suppress gram-negatives, followed by DNA extraction. Extracted DNA will be tested with a multiplex qPCR for *vanA* and *vanB*. PCR-positive samples will undergo rapid nanopore sequencing (Oxford Nanopore Technologies MinION) using a custom panel for *Enterococcus* species identification and core genome multilocus sequence typing (cgMLST). For patients with Gram-positive cocci in clusters from blood cultures, the same PCR and nanopore sequencing pipeline will be applied directly to positive blood culture broth.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, rapid diagnostic protocol for VRE that integrates into clinical microbiology workflows. It will generate data on the time-to-result benefit and the epidemiological utility of near-real-time sequencing for outbreak detection. The findings will directly inform infection prevention strategies and antimicrobial stewardship for high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While PCR for *vanA/vanB* exists, this approach innovatively combines selective broth enrichment to improve sensitivity with rapid nanopore sequencing to provide species-level confirmation and strain typing from the same sample within a single clinical day, moving beyond simple gene detection to actionable epidemiological insight.

proposals.title: A Phase IIb Randomized Controlled Trial of Daptomycin plus Ceftaroline versus Linezolid for Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecium Bacteremia in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE), particularly E. faecium, is a leading cause of bacteremia in hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, associated with high mortality and limited, often toxic, treatment options (e.g., linezolid, daptomycin monotherapy). The rise of daptomycin-non-susceptible VRE and the need for synergistic combinations to improve outcomes in this profoundly immunocompromised population represent a critical unmet clinical need pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: The combination of daptomycin plus ceftaroline will achieve superior clinical success and microbiological eradication compared to linezolid monotherapy for VRE faecium bacteremia in HSCT recipients, due to enhanced in vitro synergy and bactericidal activity.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial. Adult HSCT recipients with monomicrobial VRE faecium bacteremia will be randomized 1:1 to receive either daptomycin (10 mg/kg IV daily) plus ceftaroline (600 mg IV q8h) or linezolid (600 mg IV/PO q12h). The primary endpoint is a composite of clinical cure (resolution of signs/symptoms) and microbiological eradication at test-of-cure (7 days after end of therapy). Secondary endpoints include 30-day all-cause mortality, relapse, and safety (e.g., hematologic toxicity with linezolid, CPK elevation with daptomycin). A pre-planned pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic sub-study will inform dosing optimization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level clinical evidence for the efficacy and safety of the daptomycin-ceftaroline combination in a defined, high-risk immunocompromised population. It will generate actionable data to guide empiric and definitive therapy for VRE bacteremia in HSCT, potentially establishing a new standard of care and a framework for evaluating other novel antimicrobial combinations.
proposals.novelty: While in vitro synergy data for daptomycin plus beta-lactams against VRE exists, this trial directly translates that concept into a clinical outcome study specifically in HSCT patients, a group with a unique risk profile and infection pathophysiology, extending Dr. Deresinski's prior comparative effectiveness work on bacteremia in immunocompromised hosts.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus (VRE) Screening and Isolation Strategies in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant (HSCT) Units
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE) colonization is a major risk factor for subsequent bacteremia in immunocompromised patients, particularly in hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) units, leading to increased mortality, length of stay, and healthcare costs. Infection prevention strategies, including active surveillance testing (AST) and contact isolation, are resource-intensive, and their economic value relative to clinical benefit remains poorly quantified, creating uncertainty for hospital policy. This analysis is critical for informing cost-effective stewardship of infection control resources in high-risk settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted VRE screening and isolation policy, focused on high-risk patients upon admission to the HSCT unit, will be more cost-effective than universal screening or no screening, when considering both direct medical costs and outcomes of VRE bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will construct a decision-analytic model (Markov model) to compare three strategies: 1) universal admission screening with contact isolation for positives, 2) no routine screening, and 3) a targeted screening strategy based on prior colonization history or specific clinical risk factors. The model will simulate a cohort of HSCT patients over a one-year time horizon from the hospital perspective. Input parameters (e.g., VRE prevalence, test characteristics, isolation effectiveness, costs of screening/isolation, and costs/outcomes of VRE bacteremia) will be derived from the investigator's prior clinical studies, institutional data at Stanford, and a systematic literature review of pre-2026 evidence. The primary outcome is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) in dollars per quality-adjusted life year (QALY) gained.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative, evidence-based framework for hospital administrators and infection prevention teams to evaluate VRE control policies in transplant units. It will directly inform clinical practice by identifying the most economically efficient strategy, balancing resource use with patient safety. The model can also be adapted to assess other multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) in similar high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist for MDROs, few are specifically tailored to the unique, high-stakes environment of HSCT units where the risk and consequences of infection are extreme. This work integrates detailed clinical and microbiological parameters from the investigator's own research on bacteremia outcomes in immunocompromised hosts into a formal economic model, a novel synthesis of their expertise.

proposals.title: A Phase IIa, Randomized, Controlled Trial of Adjunctive Bacteriophage Therapy Versus Standard of Care for Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus faecium Bacteremia in Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus (VRE), particularly E. faecium, is a leading cause of bacteremia in immunocompromised patients, associated with high mortality and limited therapeutic options. The rise of multi-drug resistant strains, including those with resistance to newer agents like daptomycin and linezolid, underscores a critical need for novel, targeted antimicrobial strategies. Bacteriophage therapy offers a pathogen-specific, potentially resistance-sparing alternative, but its clinical efficacy in this high-risk population remains unproven.
proposals.hypothesis: Adjunctive administration of a pre-characterized, lytic bacteriophage cocktail targeting VRE, in combination with standard antibiotic therapy, will result in more rapid microbiological clearance of bacteremia and improved clinical outcomes compared to standard antibiotic therapy alone in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized, open-label, controlled trial. Immunocompromised adults with VRE faecium bacteremia will be randomized 1:1 to receive either standard-of-care antibiotics (e.g., daptomycin, linezolid) plus a pre-formulated, intravenous cocktail of three lytic phages with complementary host ranges, or standard-of-care alone. The primary endpoint will be time to blood culture clearance. Secondary endpoints include 30-day all-cause mortality, clinical cure rate, and safety. Serial quantitative blood cultures and phage pharmacokinetics will be performed to correlate phage levels with bacterial clearance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first controlled clinical data on the safety and efficacy of adjunctive phage therapy for VRE bacteremia in a high-risk immunocompromised cohort. It will establish a framework for integrating phage therapy into the management of multidrug-resistant Gram-positive infections and generate critical pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic data to inform future dosing regimens.
proposals.novelty: While phage therapy has been explored for some Gram-negative infections, its application against VRE in a controlled clinical trial setting, specifically within the vulnerable immunocompromised population central to Deresinski's prior work, is novel. This proposal directly translates a microbiological concept into a clinical intervention for a patient group at extreme risk of treatment failure.

proposals.title: Impact of a Pre-Transplant Outpatient Microbiome Preparation Protocol on Post-Transplant Bacteremia and Clinical Outcomes in Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: In immunocompromised patients, particularly hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) recipients, the gut microbiome is a critical determinant of infection risk and overall survival. Pre-2026 research demonstrates that inpatient antibiotic use during transplant profoundly disrupts the microbiome, increasing susceptibility to bacteremia with resistant organisms like Enterobacterales. Current microbiome-targeted interventions (e.g., selective digestive decontamination) are applied during the high-stress inpatient period, potentially too late to establish a resilient, protective microbial community before immunosuppression peaks.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, outpatient microbiome preparation protocol initiated weeks prior to HCT admission will reduce the incidence and severity of post-transplant bacteremia, particularly with antibiotic-resistant gram-negative bacilli, compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized controlled pilot study. High-risk adult patients scheduled for myeloablative allogeneic HCT will be randomized to intervention or standard care. The intervention arm will receive a multi-modal outpatient protocol for 2-4 weeks pre-admission, including: 1) Dietary counseling for a high-fiber, prebiotic diet, 2) A defined probiotic regimen (e.g., Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium strains with evidence of safety in immunocompromised hosts), and 3) Minimization of non-essential antibiotics. The primary endpoint will be the incidence of microbiologically confirmed bacteremia in the first 100 days post-transplant. Secondary endpoints will include microbiome alpha-diversity (via 16S rRNA sequencing of serial stool samples), rates of infections with piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime-non-susceptible organisms, and days of febrile neutropenia. Analysis will use intention-to-treat with chi-square and time-to-event methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective clinical data on the feasibility and efficacy of shifting microbiome modulation to the pre-transplant window. It will establish a framework for outpatient 'microbiome priming' and generate preliminary evidence on its impact on hard clinical endpoints like bacteremia and antibiotic resistance, directly informing larger multicenter trials.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of the microbiome is recognized, all clinical interventions have targeted the inpatient transplant period. This proposal innovates by testing a proactive, preventative strategy in the outpatient setting, aiming to engineer a resilient microbiome *before* the catastrophic perturbation of conditioning chemotherapy and inpatient antibiotics.

proposals.title: Clinical and Microbiological Outcomes of a Multi-Pathogen Diagnostic Protocol for Febrile Patients in Coccidioidomycosis-Endemic Regions: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: In regions co-endemic for coccidioidomycosis and other community-acquired infections, febrile patients are often managed with a single-pathogen diagnostic assumption, leading to missed concurrent infections. This is particularly critical in immunocompromised patients, where unrecognized polymicrobial or co-fungal infections increase the risk of treatment failure and mortality. Deresinski's prior work on immunocompromised hosts and anticytokine autoantibodies underscores the vulnerability of this population to complex, overlapping infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a systematic, multi-pathogen diagnostic panel for febrile patients in co-endemic areas will identify a clinically significant prevalence of concurrent infections (e.g., bacterial bacteremia plus coccidioidomycosis) that would be missed by standard care, and this missed diagnosis is associated with worse clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study at a tertiary center in a coccidioidomycosis-endemic region. Febrile, immunocompromised adult inpatients will be enrolled. The intervention arm will receive a standardized multi-pathogen diagnostic bundle upon presentation, including simultaneous blood cultures, serum (1,3)-β-D-glucan, Coccidioides antigen/antibody testing, and multiplex PCR panels for respiratory viruses and bacteria. The control arm will receive standard, clinician-directed diagnostic workup. The primary outcome will be the proportion of patients with ≥2 concurrent microbiologically confirmed infections. Secondary outcomes include time to appropriate therapy, clinical failure (a composite of persistent fever, escalation of care, or mortality), and hospital length of stay, analyzed using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence quantifying the burden and impact of concurrent infections in febrile, immunocompromised patients in a co-endemic setting. It will generate a validated diagnostic protocol that can be adopted to reduce diagnostic delay and optimize empirical therapy, directly addressing the risk of treatment failure highlighted in Deresinski's bacteremia research.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the foundational clinical assumption of a single causative pathogen, proposing a bundled, parallel testing strategy instead of a sequential, hypothesis-driven one. It integrates Deresinski's expertise in bacterial infections and fungal immunology into a unified diagnostic framework for a high-risk population.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Clinical-Microbiological Algorithm for the Simultaneous Diagnosis of Malaria and Dengue in Febrile Returning Travelers
proposals.motivation: In co-endemic regions, malaria and dengue are leading causes of febrile illness in travelers, with overlapping clinical presentations but divergent management. Pre-2026, syndromic diagnostic approaches often prioritize one disease, risking missed co-infections or delayed treatment, which can increase morbidity and mortality. This diagnostic uncertainty directly impacts patient risk and clinical outcomes, aligning with Deresinski's focus on infection-related patient risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured diagnostic algorithm mandating concurrent testing for both *Plasmodium* species and dengue virus in febrile travelers from co-endemic areas will improve the accuracy and timeliness of diagnosis, reduce inappropriate antimicrobial use, and identify a clinically significant rate of co-infections compared to standard, sequential diagnostic practices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study at a tertiary care center. Febrile adult travelers returning from defined co-endemic regions will be enrolled. The intervention is a mandatory parallel testing protocol: rapid diagnostic test (RDT) and PCR for malaria (species-specific) plus NS1 antigen and IgM/IgG serology for dengue, performed at initial presentation. We will develop a decision-tree algorithm based on presenting symptoms, travel history, and rapid test results to guide immediate empirical therapy. Diagnostic yield, time-to-diagnosis, antibiotic/antimalarial prescribing, and clinical outcomes will be compared to a historical cohort managed with sequential, clinician-directed testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical diagnostic protocol for high-risk febrile travelers, directly informing clinical practice. It will quantify the prevalence and clinical impact of malaria-dengue co-infection in this population. The study integrates clinical and microbiological data to define a clear risk-stratification strategy, reducing diagnostic failure and optimizing resource use.
proposals.novelty: While algorithms for undifferentiated fever exist, a protocol mandating *simultaneous* first-line testing for both pathogens in all eligible febrile travelers is not standard. This approach moves beyond syndromic overlap to an integrated, rule-out-both model, leveraging Deresinski's expertise in evaluating clinical failure risks associated with suboptimal antimicrobial therapy.

proposals.title: Immunomodulatory Cross-Talk in Concurrent Plasmodium vivax and Dengue Virus Infections: A Prospective Cohort Study of Clinical and Cytokine Profiles
proposals.motivation: Co-infections with Plasmodium vivax and dengue virus are increasingly recognized in overlapping endemic regions, posing a significant clinical challenge. Pre-2026 understanding suggests both pathogens can induce complex and potentially antagonistic immune responses, including cytokine storms and immunosuppression, which may alter disease severity and outcome. Investigating this interaction is critical to inform management strategies for patients presenting with acute febrile illness in co-endemic areas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that concurrent P. vivax and dengue virus infection results in a distinct, non-additive immunomodulatory state, characterized by altered pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokine dynamics (e.g., IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-10), which correlates with differential clinical outcomes compared to monoinfections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study in a co-endemic region, enrolling patients presenting with acute febrile illness. Participants will be stratified into four groups: P. vivax monoinfection, dengue monoinfection, co-infection, and other febrile illnesses (control). We will perform serial quantitative measurements of a panel of 15 cytokines via multiplex Luminex assays and correlate these with detailed clinical parameters (e.g., parasite density, viral load, platelet count, severity scores). Statistical analysis will employ multivariate regression and principal component analysis to identify unique immune signatures associated with co-infection status and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, longitudinal characterization of the host immune response during P. vivax-dengue co-infection. It will define whether the immunological interaction is synergistic or antagonistic and identify specific cytokine biomarkers predictive of disease severity. The findings will directly inform clinical risk stratification and could guide future immunomodulatory therapeutic approaches for complex co-infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Deresinski and others has focused on anticytokine autoantibodies in fungal infections or bacterial co-infection risk, the specific immunomodulatory interplay between two major tropical pathogens—a parasite and an arbovirus—remains a significant knowledge gap. This proposal integrates detailed clinical microbiology with host immune profiling, a methodological synergy reflected in the researcher's recent portfolio.

proposals.title: Clinical Impact and Risk Assessment of a Syndromic Multiplex PCR Panel for Simultaneous Detection of Malaria Parasites and Dengue Virus in Febrile Immunocompromised Patients
proposals.motivation: Fever in immunocompromised patients presents a critical diagnostic challenge, as overlapping symptoms can stem from diverse infectious etiologies, including malaria and dengue. Current diagnostic pathways often rely on sequential, pathogen-specific tests, leading to delays in appropriate therapy and potential adverse outcomes. A rapid, comprehensive diagnostic tool is urgently needed to improve the management of these high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of a syndromic multiplex PCR panel for febrile illness that simultaneously detects *Plasmodium* species and dengue virus serotypes will significantly reduce time to pathogen identification and improve antimicrobial stewardship compared to standard diagnostic methods in immunocompromised patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of immunocompromised adult patients presenting with fever. Patient samples will be tested in parallel using the BioFire® FilmArray® Global Fever Panel (or equivalent pre-2026 syndromic multiplex PCR platform) and standard-of-care diagnostics (e.g., microscopy, antigen testing, singleplex PCR). Clinical data, including time to result, antimicrobial prescriptions, and patient outcomes, will be collected. We will perform a quantitative microbial risk assessment framework, integrating diagnostic performance data to model the impact on clinical decision-making and transmission risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical validation data on the utility of a multiplex PCR panel for diagnosing concurrent or alternative malaria and dengue in a high-risk immunocompromised population. It will quantify the panel's impact on time to diagnosis and antimicrobial use, directly informing evidence-based guidelines for febrile illness management in this group.
proposals.novelty: While syndromic panels are emerging, their specific application and clinical utility assessment for the critical differential between malaria and dengue in immunocompromised hosts, integrating a risk assessment framework derived from Deresinski's prior aerosol transmission work, is novel and addresses a clear unmet need.

proposals.title: Impact of Sequential Infection Order on Clinical and Immunological Outcomes in Patients with Dengue and Plasmodium vivax Co-infection
proposals.motivation: Co-infections with dengue virus and Plasmodium vivax are common in overlapping endemic regions, but the clinical consequences are poorly understood. Pre-2026 evidence suggests immunomodulation from one pathogen can alter host response to another, potentially impacting disease severity and treatment needs. Determining if infection sequence dictates outcomes is crucial for risk stratification and clinical management in co-infected patients.
proposals.hypothesis: The order of acquisition (dengue followed by P. vivax vs. P. vivax followed by dengue) significantly alters the clinical presentation, cytokine profiles, and risk of severe complications, with the secondary infection's phenotype being modulated by the immune response to the primary infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with confirmed sequential dengue and P. vivax infections, identified via detailed travel and medical history combined with serial PCR and serological testing. Clinical data (fever duration, thrombocytopenia, hemolysis, organ dysfunction) and banked sera will be analyzed. The core analytical approach will use multivariable logistic and linear regression models, adjusting for confounders like age and comorbidities, to test for association between infection sequence and outcomes. A nested analysis will quantify anticytokine autoantibodies and pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ) using multiplex immunoassays to characterize differential immune responses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical evidence defining how infection sequence influences disease in dengue-vivax co-infections. It will generate a novel risk profile for severe presentations based on order of acquisition. The findings will inform diagnostic prioritization and anticipatory guidance for clinicians in co-endemic areas.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes co-infection prevalence, the specific 'inverse problem' of sequence impact has not been systematically studied in a clinical cohort. This integrates Deresinski's expertise in immunocompromised hosts and infection risk with a novel epidemiological and immunological framework for sequential arboviral and parasitic infections.

proposals.title: Epidemiological and Clinical Impact of Endemic Acquisition of Travel-Associated Pathogens in Immunocompromised Patients: A Multicenter Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Clinical suspicion for infections like melioidosis, strongyloidiasis, or resistant gram-negative bacteremia is often triggered by a history of recent patient travel to endemic regions. This paradigm may miss cases acquired from local, endemic reservoirs or from human-to-human transmission within healthcare settings, particularly for immunocompromised hosts who are at heightened risk for severe disease. Understanding the true burden and sources of these pathogens is critical for timely diagnosis, appropriate empiric therapy, and effective infection prevention strategies in vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of infections caused by pathogens typically associated with travel exposure (e.g., Burkholderia pseudomallei, Strongyloides stercoralis, cefoxitin-non-susceptible Enterobacterales) in immunocompromised patients are acquired through local, non-travel-related routes, including nosocomial transmission or reactivation of latent infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective multicenter cohort study of immunocompromised patients (e.g., hematologic malignancy, transplant recipients) with culture- or PCR-confirmed infections from a predefined list of 'travel-associated' pathogens from 2015-2025. For each case, we will systematically collect detailed travel history, healthcare exposures, and residential data. We will employ geospatial mapping to correlate case clusters with local environmental data and use whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of clinical isolates to compare genetic relatedness, distinguishing imported strains from locally circulating clones. Statistical analysis will compare clinical outcomes (e.g., treatment failure, mortality) between travel-linked and endemic acquisition groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantify the risk of endemic acquisition of 'travel-associated' pathogens in high-risk patients, informing revisions to clinical diagnostic algorithms and empiric therapy guidelines. It will also provide evidence for potential nosocomial transmission clusters, guiding targeted infection control interventions and environmental surveillance programs.
proposals.novelty: This study directly challenges the foundational clinical heuristic linking specific pathogens exclusively to travel history, integrating detailed epidemiology, geospatial analysis, and WGS to disentangle acquisition routes in a uniquely vulnerable population, building upon Deresinski's prior work on risk and outcomes in immunocompromised hosts with bacteremia.

proposals.title: Clinical and Microbiological Outcomes of Short-Term vs. Lifetime Antibiotic Exposure for Acute Gram-Negative Bacteremia in High-Risk Patients: A Comparative Effectiveness Study
proposals.motivation: Quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) and antibiotic stewardship often use a lifetime horizon for evaluating outcomes, which is misaligned with acute care decision-making where clinicians focus on immediate mortality and infection control. This mismatch may obscure the real-world comparative effectiveness of empiric antibiotics like piperacillin-tazobactam versus cefepime during the critical initial treatment window. A shorter, clinically relevant horizon is needed to better inform antibiotic selection for immunocompromised patients with acute bacteremia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that evaluating antibiotic efficacy using a 30-day post-bacteremia horizon, rather than a lifetime horizon, will reveal significant differences in clinical failure rates and microbiological recurrence between beta-lactam agents for treating cefoxitin-non-susceptible Enterobacterales in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of immunocompromised adults with bacteremia due to target organisms (e.g., Enterobacter cloacae, Serratia marcescens) from 2015-2025, comparing initial empiric therapy with piperacillin-tazobactam versus cefepime/carbapenems. The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality, microbiological recurrence, or escalation of therapy. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to control for confounders. A parallel analysis will model lifetime attributable risk using traditional QMRA frameworks to contrast with our 30-day endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, shorter-term outcome framework for acute infection studies, directly aligning with bedside decision timelines. It will generate comparative effectiveness data for specific antibiotic regimens in a high-risk population, informing both stewardship guidelines and risk assessment methodologies by bridging clinical and microbiological perspectives.
proposals.novelty: While Deresinski's prior work compared antibiotic regimens, it did not challenge the temporal framework of outcome assessment. This study explicitly tests the impact of the evaluation horizon itself, integrating clinical outcomes research with QMRA principles to refine the temporal scale of risk assessment for acute infections.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Universal Donor Screening versus Targeted Prophylaxis for Donor-Derived Strongyloidiasis in Non-Endemic Solid Organ Transplant Centers
proposals.motivation: Donor-derived Strongyloides stercoralis infection in transplant recipients can cause life-threatening hyperinfection syndrome. Current guidelines recommend universal screening of donors from endemic areas, but the cost-effectiveness of this approach in non-endemic recipient centers, where donor prevalence is low and prophylaxis is available, is unclear. This creates a critical clinical and economic dilemma for transplant programs, balancing resource allocation against patient risk.
proposals.hypothesis: For solid organ transplant programs in non-endemic regions, a strategy of targeted post-transplant ivermectin prophylaxis for recipients of unscreened donors is more cost-effective than universal, pre-transplant serological screening of all at-risk donors.
proposals.method: We will construct a decision-analytic Markov model comparing two strategies: 1) universal serological screening of donors from endemic regions with preemptive treatment of recipients from positive donors, and 2) no screening with universal post-transplant ivermectin prophylaxis for recipients of donors from endemic regions. Model parameters (donor prevalence, test characteristics, prophylaxis efficacy, hyperinfection mortality, costs) will be derived from pre-2026 literature and institutional data. The primary outcome is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) in dollars per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained, using a healthcare payer perspective and a lifetime horizon. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis will assess parameter uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative framework to guide institutional policy on managing Strongyloides risk in transplantation. It will directly inform clinical practice by identifying the conditions (prevalence thresholds, cost structures) under which screening or prophylaxis is economically dominant, helping programs optimize resource use without compromising patient safety.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses of screening exist, none have explicitly modeled the trade-off between universal donor screening and universal targeted prophylaxis for recipients in the specific context of non-endemic centers, integrating real-world donor flow and contemporary costs to challenge a presumed standard of care.

proposals.title: Prior Recipient Coccidioides Exposure as a Protective Factor Against Severe Donor-Derived Coccidioidomycosis in Solid Organ Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Donor-derived coccidioidomycosis (DDC) is a severe, often fatal infection in immunocompromised transplant recipients, typically managed by universal antifungal prophylaxis or screening. Current clinical paradigms assume recipients are immunologically naive, leading to uniform risk stratification. This study challenges that assumption, leveraging the established concept of endemic exposure and acquired immunity in coccidioidomycosis to potentially refine risk prediction and prophylaxis strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Solid organ transplant recipients with prior, resolved Coccidioides infection or significant endemic exposure will have a lower incidence and severity of donor-derived coccidioidomycosis compared to immunologically naive recipients, due to pre-existing protective cellular immunity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of transplant recipients who received an organ from a donor with identified Coccidioides infection or risk. Recipients will be stratified by pre-transplant Coccidioides exposure status, defined by a composite of: 1) documented prior infection, 2) positive pre-transplant serology (IgG by enzyme immunoassay or immunodiffusion), or 3) long-term residency in a highly endemic region. The primary outcome will be proven or probable DDC within one year post-transplant, with severity graded. We will use multivariable logistic regression to adjust for known confounders (immunosuppression, organ type, antifungal prophylaxis) and calculate adjusted odds ratios for DDC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence that recipient immune status modulates DDC risk, moving beyond donor-centric screening models. It could directly inform personalized post-transplant monitoring and antifungal prophylaxis guidelines, potentially sparing exposed recipients from prolonged, toxic regimens. The findings would also reinforce the importance of pre-transplant historical and serological assessment in endemic areas.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard clinical assumption of universal recipient susceptibility to DDC. While Dr. Deresinski's prior work on anticytokine autoantibodies explores host factors in fungal infection, applying the principle of naturally acquired immunity to the specific scenario of donor-derived infection in transplantation is a novel and pragmatic extension.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study to Define Optimal Antifungal Prophylaxis for Solid Organ Transplant Recipients of Organs from Donors in Endemic Areas for Histoplasmosis and Coccidioidomycosis
proposals.motivation: Solid organ transplant (SOT) recipients from donors in endemic areas for dimorphic fungi are at high risk for donor-derived fungal infections, which are associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Current guidelines are based on limited evidence, leading to variable practice regarding agent selection (e.g., fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole, isavuconazole) and duration (3-12 months). Defining an evidence-based, risk-stratified prophylaxis strategy is a critical unmet need in transplant infectious diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted antifungal prophylaxis regimen, stratified by donor endemic region, recipient immune suppression intensity, and organ type, will be associated with a lower incidence of proven/probable donor-derived endemic mycosis without increasing the rate of adverse drug events or antifungal resistance, compared to no prophylaxis or a non-stratified approach.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of SOT recipients (2015-2025) who received an organ from a donor with residence or significant exposure in histoplasmosis or coccidioidomycosis endemic areas. Primary outcome is the incidence of proven/probable donor-derived infection within one year post-transplant. Using multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching, we will compare outcomes between prophylaxis strategies. We will integrate clinical data (immunosuppression, organ type), microbiological data (donor/recipient serology, PCR), and pharmacologic data (agent, duration, levels, toxicity) to build a risk-prediction model using machine learning (LASSO regression) to identify factors associated with prophylaxis success or failure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest comparative effectiveness data to date on antifungal prophylaxis for this high-risk scenario, directly informing clinical guidelines. The derived risk-prediction model will offer a practical tool for personalizing prophylaxis decisions, aiming to maximize efficacy while minimizing toxicity and cost.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond generic recommendations by integrating donor exposure geography, recipient factors, and specific drug pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics to model prophylaxis outcomes, a precision medicine approach not yet applied systematically to this problem. It leverages the researcher's expertise in clinical mycology and risk assessment from prior work on anticytokine autoantibodies and aerosol transmission.

proposals.title: Adapting Transfusion Medicine Risk Assessment Models to Quantify Fungal Transmission Risk in Solid Organ Transplantation
proposals.motivation: Fungal infections transmitted via organ transplantation, such as those caused by *Candida* or *Aspergillus*, are associated with high morbidity and mortality, particularly in immunocompromised recipients. Current donor screening and recipient prophylaxis strategies are imperfect, and a quantitative, data-driven framework to assess per-transplant risk is lacking. The well-established, quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) models used to ensure blood product safety offer a proven template for such an analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that QMRA frameworks developed for transfusion-transmitted infections can be successfully adapted to model the risk of donor-derived fungal infection in solid organ transplant recipients, identifying key modifiable risk factors in the donor evaluation and organ handling process.
proposals.method: We will adapt the QMRA pipeline used for blood safety, integrating it with transplant-specific data. The method involves: 1) Hazard Identification: Focusing on high-risk fungi (e.g., *Candida*, *Aspergillus*, endemic dimorphic fungi). 2) Exposure Assessment: Modeling exposure dose based on pre-2026 data on donor culture positivity, organ type, preservation methods, and transport time. 3) Dose-Response Modeling: Applying existing fungal dose-response models from the literature. 4) Risk Characterization: Calculating probability of infection per transplant event using Monte Carlo simulation to account for uncertainty in inputs. The model will be informed by clinical and microbiological data from the researcher's institution and published cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first QMRA model specifically for fungal transmission in transplantation, providing a quantitative tool to stratify risk by donor, organ, and recipient factors. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on donor screening, targeted antifungal prophylaxis, and organ utilization, ultimately aiming to reduce post-transplant fungal infection rates. It also bridges methodological expertise between transfusion medicine and transplant infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: While QMRA is used in transfusion and building air safety (as in the researcher's prior work), its application to model fungal risk in organ transplantation is novel. The adaptation specifically leverages the structured risk assessment framework from another medical field to address a critical gap in transplant recipient safety.

proposals.title: Latent Class Analysis to Optimize Diagnostic Algorithms for Donor-Derived Fungal Infections in Solid Organ Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Donor-derived fungal infections (DDFIs) are a rare but devastating complication in solid organ transplant recipients, associated with high mortality. Current diagnostic pathways rely on a combination of culture, histopathology, and non-culture-based assays, but their relative performance, optimal sequence, and integration in the absence of a perfect gold standard are poorly characterized. This diagnostic uncertainty delays critical therapy and complicates the assessment of donor-derived transmission risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying latent class analysis (LCA) to a panel of imperfect diagnostic tests will reveal distinct, clinically relevant latent classes of DDFI, enabling the construction of a statistically robust, optimized diagnostic algorithm that outperforms current heuristic approaches.
proposals.method: We will perform a retrospective cohort study of solid organ transplant recipients with suspected DDFI, assembling data on diagnostic test results (e.g., culture, serum/ BAL galactomannan, serum/BAL (1,3)-β-D-glucan, PCR, histopathology). LCA will be implemented using established statistical packages (e.g., poLCA in R) to model the conditional dependence of tests and estimate the latent class (true infection status) probabilities. Model selection will be based on fit statistics (AIC, BIC) and clinical interpretability. The final model will be used to derive an algorithm maximizing sensitivity and specificity for the 'infection' latent class.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first statistically derived diagnostic algorithm for DDFI, moving the field beyond expert opinion. It will quantify the real-world performance of individual and combined tests in this specific population. The resulting framework can guide clinical decision-making and inform future prospective validation studies.
proposals.novelty: While LCA has been used in infectious diseases like tuberculosis, its application to the complex diagnostic puzzle of donor-derived fungal infections in immunocompromised hosts is novel. This approach directly integrates Deresinski's expertise in fungal infections and diagnostic risk assessment into a robust statistical framework not previously applied to this clinical question.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling of Early Coccidioides spp. Invasion in a Human Cardiac Microphysiological System Under Immunosuppressive Conditions
proposals.motivation: Disseminated coccidioidomycosis with cardiac involvement is a rare but devastating complication, particularly in immunocompromised patients, with high mortality. Pre-2026, the mechanisms of early fungal invasion and the specific host-pathogen interactions at the cardiac tissue level remain poorly understood due to the limitations of animal models and bulk-tissue analyses. This gap hinders the development of targeted strategies to prevent or treat this severe infection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early Coccidioides invasion of cardiac tissue induces a unique, dysregulated host transcriptional response at the single-cell level, which is exacerbated by immunosuppressive conditions mimicking clinical therapy, leading to failed containment and accelerated tissue damage.
proposals.method: We will establish a human iPSC-derived cardiac microphysiological system (organ-on-a-chip) to model the tissue microenvironment. This system will be infected with arthroconidia of Coccidioides posadasii under controlled flow conditions. We will apply single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics Chromium platform) to co-captured host and fungal RNA from infected chips under three conditions: immunocompetent, and with immunosuppression via dexamethasone or anti-TNF-α (reflecting Deresinski's work on anticytokine autoantibodies). Bioinformatic analysis will use Seurat for host cell clustering and trajectory inference, and custom pipelines for fungal gene expression and host-pathogen interaction mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of the initial host cardiac cell (cardiomyocytes, fibroblasts, endothelial cells) and fungal transcriptional dynamics during Coccidioides invasion. It will identify key host pathways dysregulated by immunosuppression that facilitate fungal dissemination, directly linking clinical risk factors to molecular mechanisms. The findings will offer novel targets for adjunctive host-directed therapies.
proposals.novelty: While organ-on-a-chip and scRNA-seq have been used separately, their combined application to model a deep-seated fungal infection in a specific tissue context under defined immunosuppressive regimens is novel. This approach moves beyond correlative clinical studies and bulk microbiological assays to mechanistic, single-cell causality in a human-relevant system.

ideas.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of ecological competition therapy for decolonization in high-risk neutropenic patients
ideas.description: This research performs a formal cost-effectiveness analysis comparing a novel preventative management strategy to standard care for patients at risk of febrile neutropenia. The novel strategy involves the pre-emptive application of ecological competition principles, such as using defined probiotic consortia, to decolonize patients of high-risk pathogens like CPKP prior to immunosuppression. The analysis would model the reduction in subsequent febrile neutropenia episodes, antibiotic use, and hospital costs against the upfront cost of the decolonization therapy. This synthesizes health economics with microbial ecology to assess a paradigm shift from infection treatment to proactive microbiome stewardship in vulnerable populations.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Gold Standard' Paradigm from Oncology Diagnostics
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from oncology diagnostics. In that field, the need for a traditional histopathological 'gold standard' was relaxed for certain cancers when highly specific molecular biomarkers were validated. The analogous solution was to establish these biomarkers as standalone definitive diagnostic criteria based on their predictive value for the disease state and clinical outcomes. Adapting this to the infection context, the research idea is to develop new diagnostic criteria where the key test (KT) alone can define a 'definite' infection, without mandatory SMT confirmation. This would involve validating KT against long-term patient outcomes and complication rates, rather than just test concordance, to establish thresholds or patterns where its positive predictive value is so high that SMT agreement is no longer a necessary condition for a definitive diagnosis.

ideas.title: Defining True Infection in Neutropenia: A Host-Response Validated, Machine Learning Framework for Classifying KT-Detected Organisms
ideas.description: This study establishes a composite biological standard for true infection in neutropenic hosts by prospectively integrating plasma microbial cell-free DNA (KT results) with host-response proteomics, deep immune phenotyping, and expert clinical adjudication. Using this robust standard, we will train a machine learning model on a large, multi-center cohort to classify future KT detections as probable infection, colonization, or background with a quantified probability. The resulting validated diagnostic framework will directly address the fundamental ambiguity of KT findings and provide clinicians with a actionable, evidence-based interpretation tool.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Diagnostic Integration of Single-Assay Results with Clinical Outcome Validation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a diagnostic framework that does not require a second direct microbiological test but rigorously safeguards against false positives. It defines infection certainty through a validated, quantitative relationship between a single assay's result (e.g., KT antigen level) and the subsequent clinical outcome in a well-characterized cohort. High-specificity diagnostic thresholds are derived from this clinical outcome data, ensuring that a 'definite' label reflects a high positive predictive value for true infection. This system maintains diagnostic robustness by anchoring the assay's interpretation in real-world clinical evidence rather than simply removing a confirmatory test requirement.

ideas.title: Adapt CRISPR-Cas systems for targeted plasmid clearance using phage-derived delivery and resistance-blocking mechanisms
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using CRISPR-Cas for targeted plasmid clearance by drawing an analogy from viral gene therapy and oncolytic virotherapy. In that field, viruses are engineered for selective cell targeting and armed with transgenes to overcome host defenses and enhance therapeutic effect. The adapted solution applies this two-pronged approach: first, by engineering bacteriophage delivery vehicles with tailored tropism to specifically infect bacterial hosts carrying carbapenemase plasmids, ensuring precise delivery of the CRISPR-Cas system. Second, by 'arming' these phages with genes encoding inhibitors of the bacterial host's native CRISPR-Cas immune systems (or other defensive nucleases), thereby temporarily disarming the host and preventing degradation of the therapeutic CRISPR machinery. This dual adaptation aims to significantly increase the efficiency, specificity, and success rate of plasmid clearance in complex bacterial populations.

ideas.title: Adapting Composite Endpoints from Oncology Trials to Evaluate KT
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that blood culture concordance is the primary benchmark for evaluating KT in infectious disease management. Drawing an analogy from oncology clinical trials, where the field moved from surrogate markers (e.g., tumor response) to composite clinical endpoints (e.g., Overall Survival, Progression-Free Survival) to better capture true patient benefit. The analogous solution involved defining multi-factorial endpoints that combine hard outcomes (survival) with clinical and symptomatic progression. Adapted to this research context, the solution is to develop and validate a composite clinical endpoint for KT evaluation. This endpoint would integrate microbiological data (e.g., culture/pCR concordance), clinical parameters (time to defervescence, SOFA score improvement), and healthcare utilization outcomes (avoidance of secondary therapy, length of stay). The research would involve defining the components, weighting them via expert Delphi panels akin to oncology consortia, and statistically validating the composite endpoint against long-term patient recovery and mortality, thereby providing a more clinically relevant benchmark than microbiological test concordance alone.

ideas.title: Clinical Validation of Susceptibility Restoration: A Composite Endpoint Framework for Carbapenem-Resistant Infections
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to evaluate interventions that restore carbapenem susceptibility (e.g., combination therapies, resistance-breaker adjuvants) against a composite clinical endpoint, not just microbiological concordance. The core innovation is to define therapeutic success as a combination of microbiological susceptibility restoration AND key clinical outcomes such as clinical cure, reduction in sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) score, or avoidance of rescue therapy. This moves beyond proving that an intervention can reverse resistance in vitro, to demonstrating that this reversal translates into measurable, meaningful patient benefit, thereby creating a patient-relevant benchmark for the emerging field of resistance restoration.

ideas.title: Preventive Decolonization Targeting Polymicrobial Gut Dysbiosis to Avert Subsequent Bloodstream Infections
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that specific polymicrobial KT signal patterns in the gut of high-risk, neutropenic patients are not merely markers of concurrent infection but are predictive signatures of a dysbiotic state that facilitates colonization by resistant pathogens like CPKP. By inverting the focus, the goal shifts to identifying these predictive polymicrobial 'dysbiosis signatures' to guide targeted, pre-emptive decolonization or microbiome-modulating therapies. The synergy lies in using the complex signal from Idea A as the actionable trigger for the preventive strategy from Idea B, moving clinical intervention upstream from treating polymicrobial bloodstream infections to preventing the initial pathogenic colonization that leads to them.

ideas.title: Longitudinal cfDNA surveillance of the gut mobilome to predict carbapenem-resistant K. pneumoniae bacteremia
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to longitudinally track the gut reservoir of mobile genetic elements (MGEs) carrying carbapenemase genes in high-risk patients by analyzing cell-free DNA (cfDNA) from serial blood samples collected before fever onset. By correlating the dynamics of specific plasmid or transposon signatures in pre-fever cfDNA with the subsequent development of carbapenem-resistant K. pneumoniae bacteremia during febrile neutropenia, the study aims to identify high-risk MGE profiles. This approach inverts the paradigm by using the host's cfDNA as a liquid biopsy of the gut mobilome to predict, rather than reactively diagnose, the etiology of a life-threatening infection. The synergy lies in transforming a static characterization of resistance reservoirs into a dynamic, clinically actionable predictive tool for precision antimicrobial prophylaxis and early intervention.

ideas.title: Apply Integrated Signal Deconvolution from Astrophysics to Differentiate Infection Types
ideas.description: This idea adapts signal deconvolution techniques from astrophysics, specifically radio astronomy, to the clinical problem of differentiating infection types in febrile neutropenia (FN). In radio astronomy, composite signals from multiple celestial sources are decomposed using known spectral templates to identify contributing sources. Analogously, a patient's blood presents a composite 'signal' comprising host gene expression signatures (the immune response 'background') and microbial cell-free DNA (cfDNA) fragments. The adapted solution involves creating a computational deconvolution model. This model would be trained on reference 'template' datasets: host transcriptomic signatures specific to bacterial, viral, and fungal challenges, and characteristic microbial cfDNA markers. For a new FN patient, the model would statistically decompose the mixed host-pathogen signal, weighting and integrating both data layers to output a probabilistic classification (e.g., 85% probability bacterial, 10% viral, 5% fungal), providing a more precise etiological diagnosis than either layer alone.

ideas.title: A Prospective Trial of a Clinical Decision Support System for Real-Time KT-Guided Antimicrobial Therapy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from meteorology's early warning systems for severe weather, this research adapts the solution of integrated, automated alert protocols to the problem of slow antimicrobial optimization. The analogous field solved its rapid response problem by combining real-time data streams with predictive models and automated, targeted guidance. The adapted idea is to conduct a prospective trial assessing a clinical decision support system (CDSS) designed to function as an 'antimicrobial stewardship early warning system.' This CDSS would be integrated with the EHR to automatically detect real-time KT results (e.g., rapid PCR, multiplex panels), apply interpretation algorithms, and deliver patient-specific, actionable recommendations for therapy changes (e.g., de-escalation, escalation, or cessation) directly into the clinician's workflow. The trial would rigorously evaluate whether this automated, targeted guidance leads to faster, more precise antimicrobial management compared to standard laboratory reporting.

ideas.title: Clinically-Integrated ML for Predicting Pan-Resistance and Patient-Specific Treatment Failure
ideas.description: This research fuses machine learning for predicting resistance gene combinations with the challenge to blood culture benchmarks by developing models that integrate genomic, epidemiological, and rich patient-level clinical data (e.g., host immune status, comorbidities, treatment history) to predict the risk of clinical treatment failure due to emerging pan-resistant genotypes. The model's output is not merely a predicted genotype, but a probabilistic assessment of poor clinical outcome, validated against a composite endpoint of treatment failure, recurrence, or mortality, rather than just microbiological concordance. This enables proactive, patient-specific therapeutic strategies that are optimized for both suppressing resistance emergence and achieving clinical success, moving beyond purely microbiological predictions.

ideas.title: CRISPR-Cas-based decolonization targeting plasmid-mediated resistance in polymicrobial KT infections
ideas.description: This research program aims to develop and test CRISPR-Cas systems engineered to selectively eliminate carbapenemase-encoding plasmids within the context of polymicrobial infections detected by KT in febrile neutropenia. The work will first define which specific polymicrobial KT signals correlate with clinically significant, plasmid-driven resistance that worsens outcomes. Using these clinical insights, the project will then design and validate narrow-spectrum, plasmid-targeting CRISPR antimicrobials intended not for broad eradication, but for precision 'decolonization' of high-risk resistance genes within the complex gut or infection microbiota. This approach seeks to mitigate resistance spread and improve antibiotic efficacy while preserving the protective microbiome, a strategy neither diagnostic surveillance nor standalone antimicrobial engineering could achieve.

ideas.title: Define clinical significance of polymicrobial KT signals using ecological community analysis
ideas.description: Further research is needed to determine the clinical relevance of polymicrobial KT detections in febrile neutropenia. Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology, where the significance of complex communities is deciphered using community structure analysis, this idea adapts that solution. The research would involve analyzing polymicrobial KT signals not as a simple list of pathogens but as structured microbial communities. By developing ecological metrics such as diversity indices, co-occurrence networks, and functional pathogen guilds from the KT data, and then rigorously correlating these metrics with detailed clinical outcomes, the study aims to create a predictive framework. This would define which polymicrobial profiles are benign, pathogenic, or predictive of specific complications, thereby providing a nuanced tool for clinical decision-making.

ideas.title: Molecular Inhibitors Targeting Plasmid Conjugation Machinery
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from virology—specifically the development of viral entry inhibitors—to the problem of antibiotic resistance plasmid spread. In virology, small molecules are designed to block viral attachment or fusion by targeting specific surface proteins, preventing infection initiation. The adapted idea is to develop analogous molecular inhibitors that target the bacterial conjugation machinery. These inhibitors would be designed to bind with high specificity to key surface components essential for plasmid transfer, such as the pilus tip adhesin (for initial cell-cell contact) or the Type IV Coupling Protein (T4CP, which connects the relaxosome to the transfer channel). By blocking these molecular interfaces, the inhibitor would prevent the formation of a functional mating pair or the initiation of DNA transfer, thereby 'disarming' the conjugation process. This approach aims to stop the horizontal spread of resistance genes at their source, offering a novel strategy to preserve antibiotic efficacy without directly killing bacteria, potentially reducing selective pressure for further resistance.

ideas.title: Pre-Fever cfDNA Surveillance as a Predictive Seismometer for Febrile Neutropenia
ideas.description: This idea inverts the paradigm of single-timepoint fever testing by proposing continuous, baseline cfDNA surveillance to predict febrile neutropenia (FN). Drawing an analogy from seismology's approach to earthquake prediction—where precursor signals are analyzed for risk—the solution is adapted to oncology. Instead of testing cfDNA only at fever onset, we establish a dense longitudinal cfDNA profile during stable, afebrile periods in patients undergoing myelosuppressive therapy. By analyzing shifts in cfDNA characteristics (e.g., fragmentomics, methylation, microbial reads) from this personalized baseline, the research aims to identify predictive signatures that forecast both the risk and the probable microbial etiology of a subsequent FN episode, enabling pre-emptive, targeted intervention.

ideas.title: Adapt Metagenomic Reservoir Detection from Viral Ecology to Identify Bacterial Hosts of Carbapenemase Genes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from viral ecology, where metagenomic sequencing of diverse samples has been crucial for identifying the true animal reservoirs of emerging viruses, challenging assumptions about primary hosts. The analogous solution involves using broad, untargeted metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatic analysis to find host-pathogen associations without prior bias. Adapted to antimicrobial resistance, the research would systematically sequence DNA from hospitals, wastewater, livestock, and natural environments to assemble mobile genetic elements and bacterial genomes in silico. By analyzing co-occurrence patterns, phylogenetic linkages, and abundance correlations, the study would identify which bacterial species—beyond K. pneumoniae—are significant reservoirs or vectors for spreading carbapenemase genes in different niches, fundamentally challenging the assumption of a single primary host.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework for Evaluating Knowledge Tools Against Tiered Clinical Endpoints
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple concordance or heterogeneous composites by formally modeling the causal pathway between KT use and patient outcome. It prospectively evaluates KT performance against a pre-specified, tiered clinical endpoint (e.g., time to clinical stability) within a matched cohort study. The design explicitly accounts for confounding by treatment decisions, clinician experience, and baseline severity using causal diagrams and appropriate statistical methods. This approach aims to provide robust, clinically meaningful evidence of KT impact while controlling for the non-microbiological factors that determine real-world patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Integrated Pathogen Control: A Combined Therapeutic and Environmental 'Heat-Exchange' System
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original 'Dual' concept via analogical reasoning from industrial ecology. The analogous problem of industrial waste and pollution is often solved using closed-loop systems that integrate processes to use outputs from one as inputs for another, thereby treating the 'patient' (the industrial process) and the 'environment' simultaneously. Adapting this, we propose developing integrated biomedical strategies that functionally link patient therapy and environmental decontamination in a closed feedback loop. The core idea is to engineer patient-targeted therapies (e.g., antivirals, bacteriophages, or microbiome modulators) to alter shed pathogens in a specific, detectable way. This alteration then activates or guides a complementary, targeted decontamination method in the immediate environment (e.g., light-activated antimicrobials, specific phage sprays, or enzymatic cleaners). This creates a synergistic system where treating the patient directly enhances the precision and efficacy of environmental decontamination, and vice-versa, to break transmission chains more efficiently and sustainably than independent actions.

ideas.title: Environmental Gene-Silencing Decontamination with Concomitant Patient Therapy
ideas.description: This research direction develops integrated therapeutic-decontamination platforms that combine direct, selective silencing or removal of carbapenemase genes in a patient with the targeted decontamination of their immediate environment. The core innovation is the use of gene-targeting agents (e.g., CRISPR-based biocides, sequence-specific antimicrobials) that are formulated to work both as a patient therapy and as a surface/water decontaminant. This dual application aims to clear the infection in the host while also eliminating the genetic basis of resistance from environmental reservoirs, breaking the cycle of transmission and re-colonization. The approach moves beyond killing bacteria to dismantling the resistance mechanism itself across the human-environment interface.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Optimization for KT-Guided FN Management Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from financial portfolio optimization to perform a novel cost-effectiveness analysis of KT-guided management for febrile neutropenia (FN). In the analogous field of financial economics, portfolio theory solves the problem of optimal resource allocation under uncertainty by modeling the trade-off between risk and return across a set of possible investments. We adapt this by treating the sequence of clinical decisions in FN (testing, treating, observing) as a portfolio of 'clinical actions.' Using a decision-analytic model, we will assign probabilities (e.g., of bacterial infection, KT test performance) and outcomes (clinical utility, costs) to each potential pathway. The model will then perform an optimization to identify the set of KT-guided strategies that form an 'efficient frontier'—those that maximize health outcomes for given cost constraints or minimize costs for given outcome targets—compared to standard care. This provides a rigorous, quantitative framework for identifying not just if KT is cost-effective, but the precise conditions and decision rules under which it offers optimal value.

ideas.title: Adapting Phage Display Antibody Engineering to Combat Pan-Resistant CPKP
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from oncology, where phage display antibody engineering was used to develop targeted monoclonal therapies against chemotherapy-resistant cancers. The analogous problem is pan-resistant CPKP infections, where traditional small-molecule antibiotics have failed. The adapted approach involves constructing or utilizing comprehensive phage display libraries to screen for binding agents against conserved, surface-exposed epitopes on pan-resistant CPKP isolates. By identifying high-affinity biologic binders to essential bacterial structures (e.g., porins, capsule components, or iron acquisition systems), the project aims to develop novel therapeutic entities that either exert direct bactericidal/bacteriostatic activity, potentiate the immune system's ability to clear the infection, or restore susceptibility to existing antibiotics by disrupting resistance mechanisms, thereby creating a new class of precision antimicrobials for untreatable infections.

ideas.title: Applying Environmental Forensics to Trace CPKP from Agricultural Reservoirs to Clinical Settings
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that antimicrobial resistance (specifically CPKP) is primarily a clinical problem by investigating its environmental and agricultural origins. It draws an analogy from environmental chemistry and pollution forensics, where sophisticated fingerprinting and modeling techniques are used to trace pollutants back to their sources. The adapted idea applies this forensic framework to microbial epidemiology: by systematically sampling CPKP across agricultural systems (considering factors like manure use, aquaculture, and water runoff) and linking these reservoirs to human clinical cases via high-resolution genomic and geospatial analysis, the research aims to map the transmission pathways and quantify the agricultural contribution to the clinical CPKP burden. This shifts the focus from reactive clinical management to proactive, source-targeted interventions.

ideas.title: Applying Forensic Signal-to-Noise Discrimination to KT-Organism Pathogenicity Classification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all organisms identified by KT (like metagenomic next-generation sequencing) in neutropenic hosts are pathogenic. By drawing an analogy from forensic trace DNA analysis, where the core problem is discriminating a true perpetrator's signal from pervasive background DNA, we adapt its solution. In forensics, probabilistic frameworks using control samples and quantitative thresholds solve this. Adapted to microbiology, the research would systematically establish baseline microbial DNA 'noise' from the patient's own commensal sites and their immediate environment. For each KT detection event in blood or sterile site samples, the idea is to employ quantitative metrics (e.g., genome equivalents, fragment length profiles, reads per million) and statistical modeling (e.g., likelihood ratios) against the characterized background. This creates an objective classification system to triage KT findings into probable infection, probable colonization, or probable background/contaminant, thereby improving clinical interpretation and antimicrobial stewardship in this vulnerable population.

ideas.title: Multiplexed resistance profile interrogation and targeted counter-silencing to restore antibiotic susceptibility
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond targeting a single presumed resistance gene. It integrates rapid, point-of-care genomic diagnostics to map the full complement of relevant resistance genes in a bacterial pathogen. Based on this profile, a tailored combination of gene-silencing agents is formulated to disrupt multiple resistance mechanisms concurrently. This multiplexed silencing is delivered alongside a carefully selected antibiotic, creating a therapeutic window where the pathogen's defenses are comprehensively compromised and the antibiotic can effectively clear the infection.

ideas.description: This research designs a flexible simulation framework that models multiple, parallel 'standard care' pathways and KT-integration strategies based on real-world clinical practice variation. It incorporates probabilistic sensitivity analysis and scenario testing across a wide range of epidemiological and economic inputs, including regional resistance patterns and technology costs. The output is not a single cost-effectiveness ratio, but a decision-support tool and a set of context-specific thresholds that identify when and where KT guidance is likely to be cost-effective. This approach directly addresses heterogeneity and uncertainty, providing actionable guidance for health systems rather than a potentially fragile generalized conclusion.

ideas.description: This approach moves beyond abstract ecological competition by engineering precise, molecularly-defined bacterial consortia to decolonize CPKP. It starts by screening patient microbiota for commensals with natural, targeted antagonistic mechanisms against the specific CPKP strain, ensuring a direct attack. These candidate strains are then genetically safeguarded with biocontainment features to mitigate risks of gene transfer and uncontrolled growth. The final therapeutic consortium is rigorously stress-tested in vitro against evolving CPKP populations to pre-empt treatment failure and ensure robust, durable decolonization in a clinical setting.

ideas.title: A Global Genomic Early-Warning System Using Distributed Sensor and Data Fusion Protocols
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from global earthquake early-warning systems to the challenge of scaling up genomic surveillance for Carbapenemase-Producing Klebsiella pneumoniae (CPKP). In seismology, a distributed network of sensors detects primary waves, and data is fused centrally to model the event and issue alerts. Analogously, we propose establishing a worldwide network where participating clinical and public health laboratories act as genomic sensors. They would follow standardized protocols for rapid sequencing of CPKP isolates and plasmids, transmitting essential genomic data and minimal metadata to designated regional or national bioinformatics 'fusion centers.' These centers would integrate data streams, perform real-time phylogenetic analysis and plasmid comparison, and generate a continuously updated, global situational map. This system would enable the rapid identification and reporting of emerging high-risk clones and novel resistance plasmids, functioning as an early-warning network for antimicrobial resistance.

ideas.title: Global Genomic Surveillance Network for Tracing Environmental and Agricultural Origins of CPKP
ideas.description: This research program integrates a global, real-time CPKP and plasmid surveillance network with targeted environmental and agricultural sampling and sequencing. By applying high-resolution phylogenetic and phylogeographic analysis to this combined global dataset, it will directly link clinical CPKP outbreaks to specific environmental sources, such as wastewater, livestock, or crop systems. This enables the quantification of transmission routes and the identification of high-risk agricultural practices or geographic hotspots driving resistance emergence. The synergy transforms surveillance from a reactive clinical tool into a proactive system for preventing resistance at its source, guiding environmental policy and One Health interventions.
